summary: Adversarial audit: is llmfit-dra past POC and ready to scale across a heterogeneous cluster?

dimensions.summary: The DRA plumbing itself is idiomatic — upstream resourceslice + kubeletplugin helpers, node-owned pool, correct DeviceClass CEL, attribute budget well within v1 limits, and a genuinely working publish→allocate→CDI path verified on the live Strix Halo node. But two architectural holes are disqualifying for the stated heterogeneous scale-out: device identity is an enumeration-order counter (gpu0..N) that silently remaps persisted claim allocations to different silicon after reboot/hardware change, and there is no coexistence mechanism preventing double allocation of the same GPU when a vendor DRA driver (inevitable on NVIDIA/Intel nodes) publishes it too. Secondary scale defects — hardcoded single slice (128/64-device ceiling), vendor-only llmfit pairing that mislabels mixed same-vendor GPUs, and amdgpu-only VRAM probing that advertises system RAM as dGPU memory — all specifically bite on the multi-GPU/mixed-vendor nodes the owner wants next.
validates: Pool/slice mechanics follow upstream vendor-driver precedent: resourceslice.StartController with Node owner ref and spec.nodeName (internal/publisher/publisher.go:195-206), confirmed live — slice kind-control-plane-llmfit.ai-g8mtc has the Node ownerReference, pool.name=nodeName, generation tracking (kubectl get resourceslices).

Per-device attribute budget is safe: max ~13 attributes + 1 capacity vs the 32-entry limit (k8s.io/api@v0.35.0 resource/v1 types.go:569), model values truncated to exactly DeviceAttributeMaxValueLength=64 (publisher.go:71,98,119,232-237; types.go:638), and all attribute IDs/domains within the 32/63-char limits; the resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot standardized attribute is accepted by the live v1.35 apiserver.

DeviceClass design is sound: driver-match CEL (device.driver == "llmfit.ai") per upstream convention, kind-scoped classes as a quota/admission dimension, and fit deliberately kept claim-side (deploy/deviceclass.yaml:1-51); generated claim CEL includes the healthy attribute (third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/claim.rs:129-132), so unhealthy devices are filtered without the alpha DRADeviceTaints gate.

The Update-every-cycle loop is legitimate: the helper diffs desired vs informer state so unchanged syncs issue no API writes, and the always-Update pattern self-heals externally-deleted slices (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:110-134); uevent-triggered re-probe with a ticker floor (main.go:97-107, hostNetwork wired in deploy/daemonset.yaml) is a reasonable event-driven design.

Kubelet plugin follows the upstream kubeletplugin helper contract: per-claim results so one bad claim doesn't fail the batch (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:85-91), CDI spec file doubling as idempotent prepare-state with atomic rename writes and idempotent unprepare (internal/nodeplugin/cdi.go:54-92), and mixed-claim results from other drivers correctly skipped (plugin.go:100-101).

minor.title: resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot is root-complex-granular — cannot correlate individual devices cross-driver on dense nodes
minor.evidence: Live slice (kubectl get resourceslices): gpu0 pciAddress 0000:c3:00.0 and npu0 pciAddress 0000:c4:00.1 both publish resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot: pci0000:00 — every device on this node shares one value. publisher.go:82-89 documents it as the cross-driver alignment key; README.md:75-76 calls pciAddress/pcieRoot 'correlation keys to vendor DRA drivers', but pciAddress is only published under the driver-local llmfit.ai domain (publisher.go:79-81), unusable for cross-driver matchAttribute.
minor.impact: On a typical multi-GPU server where several GPUs hang off one root complex, matchAttribute on pcieRoot aligns claims to 'same root complex', not 'same silicon' — a claim pairing an llmfit device with an nvidia device can select two different physical GPUs and still satisfy the constraint. The intra-node gpu+npu locality use (scenarios.sh:433-485) works, but the Phase-3 cross-driver identity story overstates what this attribute can do.
minor.recommendation: Document pcieRoot alignment as locality-only. For identity correlation, track upstream standardization of a per-device attribute (pciAddress) or accept that cross-driver same-device pairing is not expressible in DRA v1 and lean on the coexistence/exclusion stance instead.

minor.title: llmfit exec failure flaps device attributes, causing fleet-wide ResourceSlice churn every 30s cycle
minor.evidence: cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:150-160: on llmfit.Detect error, sys=nil and the publish proceeds with index/probe attributes — source flips llmfit→index, model/backend/bandwidth/capacity values change (publisher.go:95-128), so DevicesDeepEqual is false and the helper writes a new slice generation. deploy/daemonset.yaml runs --probe-interval=30s and execs the llmfit binary every cycle (main.go:151-159).
minor.impact: An intermittently failing llmfit (OOM under the 256Mi limit, slow exec, stale pci.ids) on many nodes produces oscillating slice updates: apiserver write load and scheduler inventory churn proportional to fleet size, plus fit decisions that differ cycle-to-cycle (a model can be schedulable at t and not at t+30s on identical hardware).
minor.recommendation: On Detect failure, keep the last-known-good llmfit.System (with a staleness bound) instead of immediately downgrading the capability source; log and export a metric for degraded cycles.

minor.title: Unified-memory RAM is published twice (cpu0 + gpu0) with no shared counter — capacity is advisory-only and double-countable
minor.evidence: Live slice: cpu0 capacity.memory 67184025927 and gpu0 capacity.memory 67184025927 — the same physical 64GB pool. publisher.go:151-155 publishes plain DeviceCapacity (no CounterSets/consumesCounters, no requestPolicy), and DRA v1 allocates whole devices; capacity is only read by fit CEL.
minor.impact: Two pods, one claiming cpu0 and one claiming gpu0 on the same APU node, are each 'promised' the full 62GiB by their fit CEL — combined they oversubscribe host RAM with no scheduler-level accounting. Also, whole-device allocation means one small model monopolizes gpu0 (no sharing), which may surprise operators expecting GPU sharing on APU nodes.
minor.recommendation: Document capacity as advisory fit input, not a reservation. Longer-term, evaluate DRA consumable capacity / counter sets (partitionable devices) to model the shared RAM pool once, referenced by both cpu0 and gpu0.

minor.title: truncate() can split a multi-byte UTF-8 rune in the model attribute
minor.evidence: publisher.go:232-237 truncate slices bytes ('s[:n]'), used for model names (publisher.go:71,98,119) against the 64-byte DeviceAttributeMaxValueLength (k8s.io/api resource/v1 types.go:638).
minor.impact: A model string with non-ASCII characters near byte 64 (possible from lspci/pci.ids names) yields an invalid-UTF-8 attribute value, which apiserver validation can reject — failing the whole slice sync for the node.
minor.recommendation: Truncate on rune boundaries (e.g. strings.ToValidUTF8 after byte-slicing, or iterate runes).

dimensions.summary: The nodeplugin's local mechanics are genuinely solid — atomic CDI writes, idempotent prepare/unprepare, per-claim error isolation, correct inventory locking, and the helper's default serialization all hold up, and scenario 8 proves cross-restart unprepare live. But it is not production-correct for the stated heterogeneous multi-GPU goal: device identity is a positional counter (gpu0/gpu1) joined by name at prepare time, so any enumeration shift silently prepares the wrong physical GPU (blocker); and the operational layer is missing — no orphaned-CDI GC, HandleError ignores the helper's explicit fatal-vs-recoverable exit contract with no liveness probe to back the 'DaemonSet restarts wedged sockets' comment, and upgrades have a per-node prepare-unavailability window (no RollingUpdate option, no maxSurge, mutable :main image pulled on every restart) on a DaemonSet that won't even schedule onto tainted GPU nodes.
validates: Atomic CDI spec writes (temp file + rename) so the runtime's watcher never sees a partial spec — internal/nodeplugin/cdi.go:56-81; unit test TestUnprepareRemovesSpecAndIsIdempotent additionally asserts no leftover files in the dir (plugin_test.go:203-207)

Prepare and Unprepare are idempotent and crash-tolerant for the common restart case: rewrite yields identical spec/CDI IDs (TestPrepareIsIdempotent, plugin_test.go:131-143), missing file on unprepare is success (cdi.go:86-92), and cross-restart unprepare by a fresh instance is exercised live (hack/scenarios.sh:308-336, scenario 8 passes on the kind cluster)
Per-claim error isolation in batched Prepare — one bad claim doesn't fail the batch (plugin.go:85-91; TestPrepareUnknownDeviceFailsThatClaimOnly, plugin_test.go:145-163)

CDI device names embed the claim UID exactly as the helper docs recommend to defeat runtime CDI cache false hits (cdi.go:19-21, plugin.go:109; draplugin.go:90-95 'A DRA driver can use the claim UID for it')

No in-process concurrency hazard on CDI writes or inventory: the helper serializes all prepare/unprepare gRPC calls by default (draplugin.go:433-436, 542 `serialize: true`, 818-840), the Inventory RWMutex correctly guards the re-probe writer vs. readers (plugin.go:34-58), and the inventory is populated before the kubelet plugin registers (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:76-89) so there is no empty-inventory startup window
Graceful shutdown wiring is correct for clean deregistration: signal-bound context plus deferred helper.Stop (main.go:70-71, 88-94), which the helper requires for socket cleanup
Live cluster state is healthy: driver pod 1/1 Running, one ResourceSlice published for kind-control-plane, `go test ./internal/nodeplugin/` passes

minor.title: No last-line-of-defense check for double-allocated devices
minor.evidence: Helper contract (draplugin.go:76-81): 'A DRA driver should verify that all devices listed in a [DeviceRequestAllocationResult] are not already in use for some other ResourceClaim ... the DRA driver is the last line of defense against using the same device for two different unrelated workloads.' internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:93-126 prepareClaim writes a spec for whatever the allocation says, with no tracking of which device names are already prepared for other claim UIDs (the per-claim CDI files on disk would make this check trivial).
minor.impact: A scheduler/resourceslice race (two pools/generations during the rename windows from the blocker finding, or an upstream allocator bug) results in two pods sharing an 'exclusive' GPU/NPU silently — for LLM workloads that is OOM/perf corruption with no diagnostic.
minor.recommendation: At prepare time, scan existing llmfit.ai-*.json specs (or an in-memory prepared-set rebuilt from them at startup) and fail the claim if a requested device name is already prepared for a different live claim UID.

minor.title: Prepare failure on vanished device relies on kubelet retry with no requeue/health feedback
minor.evidence: internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:104-108 returns a per-claim error when the allocated device is missing from the current inventory. Correct behavior, but the claim stays allocated to dead hardware; nothing deallocates it (the taint path is opt-in and gated, main.go:39 `--publish-taints=false` default).
minor.impact: Pods whose device was hot-removed sit in ContainerCreating retry loops indefinitely rather than being rescheduled — noticeable churn at scale on hotplug-heavy nodes.
minor.recommendation: Document/alert on this state; consider enabling device taints or claim deallocation once DRADeviceTaints is available in the target clusters.

dimensions.summary: The driver is truthful and functional on the single AMD-APU node it was built on, but its heterogeneity story is largely aspirational: DRM-class enumeration plus amdgpu-specific sysfs reads means NVIDIA and Intel discrete GPUs get wrong capacity (system RAM), wrong unifiedMemory (true), and — for NVIDIA — CDI edits that cannot run CUDA (no /dev/nvidia*, no driver libs), while the index still publishes bandwidth for H100/L40S/4090/5090 so the scheduler will happily place doomed pods there. The README's coexistence stance ("vendor event streams are the vendor DRA driver's job on nodes where one runs") has zero code behind it: no vendor-driver detection, no defer/refuse, no DaemonSet node filtering, so mixed clusters running gpu.nvidia.com double-offer the same silicon. Multi-GPU render-node pairing is genuinely correct, but device naming is positional and renumbers under hotplug, and llmfit-to-probe pairing collapses mixed same-vendor GPU nodes.
validates: Render-node pairing is done via the per-device sysfs drm/ subdir, not card-index arithmetic, so cardN/renderD12x pairing survives multi-GPU enumeration where minors don't line up — probe.go:207-218, explicitly tested for the 2-GPU case at internal/probe/probe_test.go:115-116 ('must pair via sysfs drm/, not card index').

NPU enumeration is vendor-generic: any /sys/class/accel entry (intel_vpu, amdxdna, and future qaic/habana accel-class drivers) is picked up with the devtmpfs-convention /dev/accel/accelN node — probe.go:116-120, 175-177. Confirmed live: npu0 (amdxdna, 0000:c4:00.1) published on the kind node.

CPU-only nodes are handled truthfully: cpu0 is always published (probe.go:122-126), prepares env-only with no device nodes (plugin.go editsFor: DevNode empty for KindCPU), and CI exercises this path (README scenario 6).

The generated fit CEL requires memoryBandwidthGBs AND healthy (third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/claim.rs:129-133), so unindexed hardware, BMC framebuffers, and virtio displays — which get no bandwidth attribute — are not selectable by llmfit-generated claims. This containment only holds for claims that use the generated CEL, not the bare gpu.llmfit.ai DeviceClass (deploy/deviceclass.yaml selects only kind=='gpu').

Provenance is honest: source=llmfit|index|probe attribute per device (publisher.go:97,118,125), verified live — gpu0 source=llmfit (Vulkan backend), npu0 source=index (llmfit doesn't model XDNA), matching the README's stated fallback chain.
pciAddress/pcieRoot plus the standardized resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot are published (publisher.go:79-89), giving claims a real cross-driver matchAttribute correlation key.
Walk output is sorted by kind+index (probe.go:128-133) so unchanged hardware compares deep-equal across probe cycles, and driverUnbound health is a real fact check (probe.go:64-75).

minor.title: SR-IOV VFs and PFs are indistinguishable: every VF publishes as a full GPU with full-index capability
minor.evidence: grep -rn 'physfn|sriov|virtfn' internal/ returns nothing; probe.go:140-182 walkClass treats every drm card identically, reading vendor/device/driver from the (VF's) device dir. No check of the 'physfn' symlink that marks a VF.
minor.impact: On Intel Flex/Xe or amdgpu SR-IOV nodes, each VF appears as an independent gpuN. If the VF's PCI device ID matches an index entry, each VF advertises the full card's bandwidth; VRAM (already broken per the amdgpu-only probe) defaults to systemRAM. N VFs multiply advertised capacity N-fold, and the PF is also published alongside its own VFs — claiming the PF while VFs are in use is invalid.
minor.recommendation: Detect VFs via the physfn symlink; either skip them, or publish them with a vf=true attribute, fractional/unknown capacity, and suppress the PF when VFs are enabled.

minor.title: Non-modeled accel-class NPUs (Habana Gaudi, Qualcomm qaic, etc.) publish false unifiedMemory and system-RAM capacity
minor.evidence: probe.go:116 walks /sys/class/accel generically; mem_info_vram_total (probe.go:166) never exists for accel devices, so VRAMBytes=0 → UnifiedMemory()=true (probe.go:83-85) → publisher.go:127 'unifiedMemory: d.UnifiedMemory()' and publisher.go:131-137 capacity=systemRAM on the probe path. data.json has exactly two NPU entries (8086:643e, 1022:17f0). Live slice confirms the pattern is capacity=systemRAM even for the known amdxdna npu0 (65606144Ki = host RAM).
minor.impact: A Gaudi2/3 (dedicated 96GB HBM, accel-class in newer kernels) would publish unifiedMemory=true with capacity = host RAM — factually wrong on both axes. No bandwidth attribute means fit CEL won't select it, but the bare npu.llmfit.ai DeviceClass will, and prepare injects only /dev/accel/accelN, which is insufficient for Habana stacks. Additionally on APUs, gpu0 and npu0 each advertise the full shared 64GB pool (seen live), double-counting one physical memory pool across two allocatable devices.
minor.recommendation: For unindexed accel devices, publish no memory capacity and unifiedMemory only when positively known; add a shared-pool attribute (or divide/annotate) so APU gpu+npu claims can't jointly promise 2x the physical RAM.

minor.title: BMC/virtual framebuffers on 'CPU-only' servers publish as healthy GPUs selectable via the bare gpu.llmfit.ai class
minor.evidence: probe.go:140-182 publishes any drm card (ASPEED ast 1a03, mgag200, virtio); publisher.go:227-228 default vendor 'pci-<id>'; publisher.go:144-149 taints only unhealthy devices, and an ast BMC with bound driver is healthy=true with capacity=systemRAM. deploy/deviceclass.yaml gpu.llmfit.ai selects only kind=='gpu'. README acknowledges these appear on CI runners and relies on fit CEL's bandwidth requirement to avoid them (claim.rs:131).
minor.impact: Fleet-wide, every rack server with a BMC framebuffer stops being 'CPU-only': it advertises a healthy gpu0 with host-RAM capacity. Any claim using gpu.llmfit.ai with a capacity-only or no selector allocates the BMC and gets /dev/dri nodes of a 2D framebuffer. Also pollutes inventory dashboards and quota counted by DeviceClass.
minor.recommendation: Filter or demote display-class-only devices (no render node is a strong signal: renderNode()=='' for ast/mgag200) — e.g. publish fitCapable=false and exclude them from gpu.llmfit.ai's class selector.

dimensions.summary: The steady-state control-plane story genuinely scales: per-node no-op syncs cost zero API writes and each daemon's watch is field-selector-scoped to its own slices, so 500 nodes is 500 idle watches, not O(nodes^2). The failure modes do not scale: the llmfit-generated fit CEL hard-errors (not just non-matches) on any kind=gpu/npu device lacking memoryBandwidthGBs — which the publisher deliberately emits for unindexed/virtual display adapters — knocking entire nodes (including real GPU servers with BMC framebuffers) out of allocation; the DaemonSet has no priorityClassName or tolerations; the hotplug listener dies permanently on netlink overrun exactly during the storms it exists for; and device names are enumeration-order counters that silently remap /dev nodes across hot-remove.
validates: No-op probe cycles cost zero API writes at any fleet size: main.go:133 controller.Update only queues a pool sync (resourceslicecontroller.go:335-357); syncPool reads the informer cache and issues Update/Create only on semantic diff (resourceslicecontroller.go:593, 728-741, 772-798). Live pod log confirms 20+ min of 30s cycles with no slice writes (only 'llmfit capability assessment' lines). 500 nodes x 2/min = ~17 queue-adds/s cluster-wide, all local CPU.

Per-daemon watch load is O(own slices), not O(cluster): the helper's informer list/watch uses a field selector on driver name AND spec.nodeName (resourceslicecontroller.go:443-452), so 500 driver pods = 500 cheap filtered watches plus one cached Nodes().Get for the owner UID (resourceslicecontroller.go:630-639).

Slice GC on node deletion works: publisher.Start sets Owner{APIVersion:v1, Kind:Node, Name:nodeName} (publisher.go:195-206) and syncPool stamps a controller OwnerReference with the node UID on every created slice (resourceslicecontroller.go:820-833 region), so kube GC reaps slices with the Node object; KCM's resourceslice GC is a backstop.

Scheduler-side CEL cost is bounded: the generated fit expression is 3 conjuncts (claim.rs:129-132), compiled once per unique string via celCache (allocator_stable.go:959) with per-attempt memoization of device-vs-request results (allocator_stable.go:914-917), and the DeviceClass kind pre-filter (deploy/deviceclass.yaml) short-circuits before the claim selector for non-matching kinds. ~3 devices/node keeps Filter cost trivial at 500 nodes.

Probe output ordering is deterministic (probe.go:128-133 sorts by kind,index), and slice capacity comparison is semantic (DevicesDeepEqual uses apiequality.Semantic, resourceslicecontroller.go:1035-1045), so quantity-format differences (live cluster shows '67184025927' vs '65606144Ki') do not cause write flapping.

llmfit failure degrades instead of failing publish: Detect errors fall back to the embedded index (main.go:151-160), and a failed re-probe keeps the previous inventory rather than wiping slices (main.go:119-122). Measured llmfit exec cost on the live Strix Halo node is ~130-220ms per 30s cycle (log timestamps 11:10:50.136 ... 11:30:20.165 offset from tick).

minor.title: 256Mi memory limit shared with the llmfit child under a 64Mi request; 10m CPU request for a 30s exec loop
minor.evidence: deploy/daemonset.yaml:55-60 (requests cpu:10m/memory:64Mi, limits memory:256Mi); the llmfit subprocess (and any vendor tools it shells to) runs inside the same cgroup every 30s (main.go:152-159, llmfit.go:47). Measured fine on the single AMD node (~200ms cycles), but unvalidated on NVIDIA multi-GPU nodes where nvidia-smi/driver-init RSS is materially larger.
minor.impact: An OOM kill of the container takes down the kubelet plugin with the full eviction consequences (stuck Prepare/Unprepare) from the priority finding; usage persistently above the 64Mi request also makes the pod the top eviction candidate. Burstable QoS plus a hard limit is the worst combination for a node-critical agent.
minor.recommendation: Measure driver+llmfit peak RSS on the largest node profile, set requests == limits (Guaranteed), and consider running llmfit detection less often (on probe diff or a longer cadence) rather than every 30s on every node including CPU-only ones.

minor.title: Mutable :main image tag with imagePullPolicy Always: fleet version skew and registry-coupled restarts
minor.evidence: deploy/daemonset.yaml:29-30: `image: ghcr.io/sympozium-ai/llmfit-dra:main`, `imagePullPolicy: Always`, plus imagePullSecrets (line 18-19) implying a private GHCR repo with rate limits.
minor.impact: At 500 nodes, pods restarting at different times pull different builds of :main — mixed driver versions publishing potentially different attribute schemas against the same DeviceClasses/claims. Pull-policy Always also couples every driver restart (including eviction recovery) to GHCR availability and rate limits; a registry outage prevents the node-critical plugin from coming back.
minor.recommendation: Pin an immutable version tag or digest in the manifest (CI already builds tagged images), and use IfNotPresent.

minor.title: Per-node driver ServiceAccount can mutate every node's ResourceSlices
minor.evidence: deploy/rbac.yaml ClusterRole grants resourceslices create/update/patch/delete cluster-wide to the single shared ServiceAccount; the resourceslice helper only needs the pod's own node's slices (it filters its informer to spec.nodeName, resourceslicecontroller.go:443-452).
minor.impact: One compromised node (the pod is privileged, hostPID, hostNetwork — daemonset.yaml:22-25,39-40) can delete or forge inventory for all 500 nodes, redirecting scheduling cluster-wide. The kubelet NodeRestriction admission does not cover custom ServiceAccounts.
minor.recommendation: Add a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy binding slice writes to the requesting pod's node (as upstream DRA drivers document), or move toward per-node credentials.

dimensions.summary: The test story proves a real, well-built single-node happy path (publish -> CEL allocation -> CDI prepare -> pod Running -> unprepare) on exactly two configurations: one AMD Strix Halo box, and a GPU-less kind node where every GPU scenario skips. It provides essentially zero evidence for "heterogeneous scale": no multi-node scheduling, no NVIDIA/Intel GPU ever runs end-to-end, no consumer pod ever executes real compute (assertions stop at env vars and /dev node existence), and there are no reboot/name-stability, contention, upgrade, or negative tests — the suite is itself structurally single-node, so parts of it become tautological or flaky on the very cluster shape the owner wants next. "No longer a POC" is defensible for single-node DRA plumbing; "validated for heterogeneous scale" is not supported by this evidence.
validates: E2E genuinely exercises the full DRA pipeline against a real API server/kubelet, not mocks: CEL allocation asserted via claim status (hack/scenarios.sh:202-210), pod Running proves NodePrepareResources worked (scenarios.sh:213-214), per-claim CDI file existence/removal checked on-disk (scenarios.sh:296-306).

Scenario 8 is a real crash-consistency test of the 'CDI file is the only state' design: a claim prepared by the OLD driver instance is unprepared by the NEW instance after rollout restart, then a fresh claim prepares (scenarios.sh:313-336).

Scenario 11 validates cross-device matchAttribute alignment via resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot with two devices merged into one pod's CDI on real hardware (scenarios.sh:440-485); the attribute is actually published (internal/publisher/publisher.go:88).

Unit tests are substantive: probe_test.go builds a faithful fake sysfs (2 GPUs + NPU + connector/render entries that must be skipped) and verifies render-node pairing survives non-aligned minors (internal/probe/probe_test.go:26-28,105-117); plugin_test.go covers idempotent prepare/unprepare, per-claim failure isolation, foreign-driver result skipping, and no temp-file leakage (internal/nodeplugin/plugin_test.go:131-208); publisher_test.go checks DRA attribute-count and 64-char limits (publisher_test.go:92-104).
CI runs the full e2e on a kind cluster for every PR (cpu0 fallback path) with real image build and failure diagnostics (.github/workflows/build.yml:34-73); go test ./... passes locally today.
Scenario 6 contains one genuine negative assertion: a cpu0 claim must NOT inject GPU device nodes (scenarios.sh:284).

minor.title: Weak/tautological assertions inside the scenarios that do run
minor.evidence: Scenario 2 gates HAS_GPU on `.attributes.memoryBandwidthGBs.int > 0` (scenarios.sh:109) and then asserts `[ "$bw" -gt 0 ]` (line 117) — true by construction. Scenario 2 also hard-requires `indexed.bool == true` (line 114), so a new GPU that llmfit prices but data.json doesn't know FAILS the suite — punishing exactly the unknown-hardware case heterogeneity brings. Scenario 5 verifies `source == "llmfit"` (lines 227-238), i.e. the driver's self-reported attribute; it distinguishes fallback-vs-llmfit but validates none of llmfit's numbers against ground truth. Scenario 12 asserts only a driver log line count increased (lines 494-506).
minor.impact: Green runs overstate coverage: bandwidth positivity is circular, the index requirement makes the suite brittle on new hardware rather than protective, and 'hotplug works' means 'a log line appeared'.
minor.recommendation: Drop the duplicated bw check; relax scenario 2 to accept source==llmfit with indexed==false (warn, don't fail); make scenario 12 assert an observable slice change; where possible cross-check one published number against an independent source (e.g. sysfs mem_info_vram_total vs published capacity).

minor.title: Unit-test blind spots: cmd/ has no tests, hotplug Listen and the reconcile loop are untested, single-slice 128-device limit unguarded
minor.evidence: `go test ./...` reports 'no test files' for cmd/llmfit-dra. hotplug tests cover only parseUevent/relevant (internal/hotplug/hotplug_test.go, 39 lines); Listen's socket loop, debounce, and channel-coalescing (hotplug.go:61-107) are untested. main.go's run loop — desiredState error handling ('keeping previous inventory', main.go:119-122), the always-Update self-heal (main.go:124-134), llmfit-failure fallback (main.go:151-160) — has no unit coverage; publisher.Start is never tested against a fake client. BuildResources puts ALL devices in one slice (publisher.go:183-191) with no test or guard for the DRA 128-devices-per-slice API limit.
minor.impact: The loop's degraded modes (llmfit binary broken on some distros, uevent socket unavailable, probe errors on unusual kernels) are exactly the varying-kernel heterogeneity risks, and they are exercised only implicitly by the happy-path e2e. A node with >128 published devices would fail API validation with no test to say so.
minor.recommendation: Extract the reconcile loop into a testable function with fake clock/prober; test llmfit-fails-then-recovers and probe-error-keeps-previous-inventory; add slice chunking (or an explicit cap with a test) in BuildResources.

minor.title: CI validates one K8s version, one kernel, one arch — 'varying kernels' claim has no test dimension
minor.evidence: CI pins kindest/node:v1.35.0 only (.github/workflows/build.yml:54); images build linux/amd64 only (build.yml:104-108); the live node is a single Fedora kernel (kubectl get nodes -o wide: 7.0.12-101.fc43). No matrix over K8s versions (DRA GA'd at 1.34; no skew testing), kernels, or cgroup/containerd versions; scenarios-cpu.sh is never invoked by CI.
minor.impact: sysfs layout differences (mem_info_vram_total is amdgpu-specific, probe.go:166; ras/ue_count likewise, probe.go:168), older containerd CDI support, and API skew across a mixed fleet are all unprobed dimensions.
minor.recommendation: Add a small CI matrix (K8s 1.34/1.35/next kind images), and document per-kernel expectations for the probe's amdgpu-specific sysfs reads; treat Intel/NVIDIA sysfs fixtures for VRAM/RAS as required unit additions.

dimensions.summary: The DaemonSet runs privileged + hostPID + hostNetwork, but only hostNetwork is actually required by a code path (the NETLINK_KOBJECT_UEVENT socket in hotplug.go); privileged and hostPID are over-granted relative to what the Go probe and llmfit binary touch. The RBAC is the more serious scaling risk: a cluster-wide resourceslices create/update/delete bound to a single ServiceAccount, with no per-node authorization, means any compromised node's (privileged) driver pod can forge or delete every other node's inventory — a cluster-wide scheduling-manipulation blast radius that grows with node count. The GHCR pull-secret flow passes a classic PAT (ghp_) on the command line and CDI device-node paths are injected without allowlisting.
validates: hostNetwork is genuinely required: hotplug.go:62-70 opens an AF_NETLINK/NETLINK_KOBJECT_UEVENT socket and binds Groups:1, which is network-namespace scoped, and main.go:102-106 degrades gracefully (ticker-only) if Listen() fails, so the dependency is real and non-fatal.

The netlink listener binds Groups:1 (kernel uevents), hotplug.go:66-67, not group 2 (udevd rebroadcast); kernel multicast to group 1 requires CAP_NET_ADMIN to join, so a fully unprivileged userspace process cannot trivially spoof these events onto the socket.

CDI specs are written atomically (temp file + os.Rename, cdi.go:68-80) so the runtime's fsnotify watcher never reads a partial spec, and removeSpec treats already-gone as success (cdi.go:86-92), making Unprepare idempotent.

ResourceSlices use an Owner of kind Node (publisher.go:199-203) so slices are garbage-collected with the node, and Prepare re-validates that an allocated device is still in the live probe inventory before writing a spec (plugin.go:103-108), closing a stale-allocation gap.
sysfs is mounted read-only (daemonset.yaml:42-44), so the probe's inputs (device node paths derived from sysfs symlinks) cannot be rewritten from inside the driver container itself.
CI grants only contents:read + packages:write and uses the ephemeral per-run GITHUB_TOKEN for GHCR (build.yml:9-11,88-89), not a long-lived PAT.

minor.title: hostPID: true is not used by any code path
minor.evidence: daemonset.yaml:22 sets hostPID:true with a comment claiming llmfit needs the host PID namespace. No Go code reads the host process table, and the llmfit `--json system` detection path uses sysfs, vendor CLIs, and /proc/meminfo|cpuinfo (hardware.rs) which are namespace-independent for meminfo/cpuinfo. The only sysinfo::System::new_all() process scan (providers.rs:2121) is in the LM Studio GGUF-download budget helper, not the system-assessment path invoked by internal/llmfit (llmfit.go:47 runs `--json system`).
minor.impact: hostPID exposes the full host process table (and via /proc, host process env/cmdline that may carry secrets) to the container, widening what a compromised driver pod can read on every node, for no functional benefit.
minor.recommendation: Remove hostPID:true. If a future llmfit code path genuinely needs host process visibility, gate it and document the exact call.

minor.title: CDI device-node paths are injected without allowlisting, and /var/run/cdi is a shared host injection surface
minor.evidence: editsFor() (plugin.go:137-163) copies d.RenderNode/d.DevNode straight into cdiDeviceNode paths and unconditionally adds /dev/kfd for any amdgpu device (plugin.go:159-161); these come from sysfs reads (probe.go:173-177,207-217) with no validation that the path is an expected /dev/dri or /dev/accel node. /var/run/cdi is a hostPath DirectoryOrCreate mount (daemonset.yaml:76-79) created 0o755 (cdi.go:57). Any other privileged workload on the node — or a driver bug — can drop a CDI spec there that the runtime will honor for consumer pods.
minor.impact: CDI specs are trusted by containerd/CRI-O to mount arbitrary device nodes into consumer containers. Without a path allowlist, a poisoned sysfs symlink (needs host compromise) or any co-located writer of /var/run/cdi could inject /dev/mem, /dev/kmsg, or another device into unprivileged consumer pods, breaking pod isolation. Low likelihood given sysfs is read-only, but there is no defense-in-depth.
minor.recommendation: Allowlist injected device-node paths to expected prefixes (/dev/dri/, /dev/accel/, /dev/kfd) and reject anything else in editsFor/writeSpec. Document that /var/run/cdi must not be writable by other pods, and consider a more restrictive dir mode.

minor.title: uevent parser does not verify the netlink message originates from the kernel (nl_pid==0)
minor.evidence: hotplug.go:82-93 reads from the group-1 socket and hands the buffer straight to parseUevent without inspecting the source SockaddrNetlink (unix.Recvfrom would expose nl_pid). Binding to group 1 does keep out unprivileged senders, but with hostNetwork any other CAP_NET_ADMIN process in the host netns could unicast/multicast a forged 'change@...SUBSYSTEM=drm' message that passes relevant() and triggers a re-probe.
minor.impact: Bounded: a forged uevent only causes an extra sysfs re-walk (debounced to 2s, main.go:102). Worst case is minor re-probe amplification/DoS, not spec forgery, since the re-probe re-reads read-only sysfs. Included for completeness of the spoofing vector.
minor.recommendation: Use unix.Recvfrom and drop messages whose source nl_pid != 0 (non-kernel). Cheap hardening that closes the forgery path entirely.

minor.title: Test/side-load tooling spawns privileged hostPID pods and remounts host /sys rw via nsenter -t 1
minor.evidence: Makefile:28-35 `sideload` creates a privileged hostPID busybox in kube-system and pipes `docker save` through `kubectl exec ... nsenter -t 1 -m -- ctr images import`. scenarios.sh:493-499 runs `driver_exec nsenter -t 1 -m -- sh -c 'mount -o remount,rw /sys; echo change > .../uevent; mount -o remount,ro /sys'` to synthesize a uevent, entering the host mount namespace and temporarily making host sysfs writable.
minor.impact: These are operator/CI-only paths, not the runtime, so production impact is nil — but they normalize nsenter-to-PID-1 and rw-remount-of-host-sysfs patterns, and the sideload pod is a standing privileged escape primitive in kube-system if left running. On a shared/heterogeneous cluster these scripts should not be run against production nodes.
minor.recommendation: Guard these targets behind an explicit opt-in var, ensure the image-loader pod is always torn down (it is deleted --wait=false, so a failed run can leak it), and document that scenarios.sh mutates host mount state and must only run on disposable test nodes.

confirmed.title: No coexistence guard: same GPU allocatable by llmfit.ai and a vendor DRA driver simultaneously (double allocation)
confirmed.evidence: internal/probe/probe.go:107-135 walks ALL of /sys/class/drm and /sys/class/accel with no vendor exclusion; internal/publisher/publisher.go:40-159 publishes every probed device as a full allocatable DRA device (not attribute-only). The only cross-driver mechanism is opt-in claim-side alignment: publisher.go:82-89 publishes 'resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot' "lets a single claim align our device with a vendor driver's via constraints.matchAttribute". README.md:243 admits live cross-driver matchAttribute is untested ("needs a mixed NVIDIA/Neuron node"). Nothing in the repo (no flag, no config, no probe filter) prevents publishing a GPU that gpu.nvidia.com / gpu.intel.com also publishes.
confirmed.impact: On the planned heterogeneous cluster, NVIDIA/Intel nodes will run vendor DRA drivers (required for MIG, NVML health, etc.). The scheduler treats devices from different drivers as independent inventory: pod A claims the H100 via gpu.nvidia.com, pod B claims the same silicon via llmfit.ai, both allocate, both kubelet plugins inject the same /dev nodes, and the GPU is silently oversubscribed. matchAttribute is per-claim cooperation, not exclusion — it cannot prevent two unrelated claims from double-booking. This breaks the core DRA invariant (exclusive device allocation) fleet-wide.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick an explicit coexistence stance before scale-out: (a) per-node/per-vendor exclusion config so llmfit-dra skips publishing devices whose kernel driver indicates a vendor DRA driver is authoritative (e.g., --exclude-vendors=nvidia or detect the vendor plugin socket under /var/lib/kubelet/plugins/), publishing attribute-only correlation data instead is not possible in DRA v1 — so skip entirely; or (b) position llmfit.ai as the sole allocator on nodes without vendor drivers (AMD APU/NPU/CPU-only) and document that mixed operation on the same silicon is unsupported. Enforce via node labels + DaemonSet nodeAffinity.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line. probe.go:107-135/140-182 walks all /sys/class/drm and /sys/class/accel entries with zero vendor/driver filtering; publisher.go:40-159 publishes each probed device as a full allocatable DRA device with Capacity (no attribute-only mode exists). Checked for mitigations and found none: cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:31-39 has no exclusion flag; deploy/daemonset.yaml has no nodeAffinity/nodeSelector, so the DaemonSet lands on every node including future vendor-driver nodes; internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:100 skips foreign-driver allocation results but only within a single claim (TestPrepareSkipsForeignDriverResults, plugin_test.go:165-178) — it cannot prevent a second, unrelated claim allocating the vendor driver's copy of the same GPU. The pcieRoot attribute (publisher.go:82-89) is per-claim matchAttribute cooperation, not exclusion; the DRA scheduler tracks allocation per (driver,pool,device) with no cross-driver dedup, so the same silicon published by gpu.nvidia.com/gpu.intel.com and llmfit.ai is two independent allocatable entries — double allocation and dual /dev injection (llmfit-dra injects /dev/dri/cardN+renderD, /dev/kfd per plugin.go:159) is real. README.md:51-52,75,243 confirms the repo intends coexistence with vendor drivers (pcieRoot as 'correlation keys to vendor DRA drivers') yet admits cross-driver behavior is untested and documents no exclusion stance. Guaranteed on Intel/AMD discrete nodes (i915/xe/amdgpu always register DRM cards); on NVIDIA it depends on nvidia_drm being loaded, which GPU Operator typically does. Latent on today's single-node AMD cluster, but for the stated heterogeneous scale-up goal (vendor DRA drivers required for MIG/NVML) it breaks the DRA exclusive-allocation invariant fleet-wide, so blocker stands. Recommendation (exclusion flag or nodeAffinity + documented stance) is sound; the finding's aside that attribute-only publishing is impossible in DRA v1 is consistent with the API (every published device is allocatable).

confirmed.title: Device names (gpu0..N) are enumeration-order counters — allocated claims silently bind to the wrong physical device after reboot/hardware change
confirmed.evidence: internal/probe/probe.go:150-180: 'idx := 0' then 'Index: idx' / 'idx++' per matching /sys/class/drm entry in os.ReadDir (lexicographic) order; probe.go:78-80 Name() = fmt.Sprintf("%s%d", d.Kind, d.Index). The name is NOT derived from PCI address, UUID, or even the kernel's cardN number — it is a dense counter over whatever entries exist at probe time. internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:103 'dev, ok := p.inv.lookup(alloc.Device)' joins the persisted claim allocation result back to hardware purely by this name; plugin.go:104-108 only errors if the name is absent, not if it now denotes different silicon.
confirmed.impact: ResourceClaim allocation results persist in claim.status across node reboots. Kernel DRM card numbering changes with async driver probe order, module load order, simpledrm→real-driver handoff, and hardware add/remove; removing/adding one card shifts every subsequent index (a hot-removed gpu0 renames gpu1→gpu0). On a multi-GPU heterogeneous node a claim allocated to 'gpu1' (the H100) can, after reboot, prepare 'gpu1' (now the iGPU) — wrong /dev nodes injected with no error, and two claims can land on one physical GPU. Also on ≥11-GPU nodes lexicographic ReadDir order (card1, card10, card2) makes index↔card mapping non-obvious. Vendor precedent (NVIDIA UUID-derived names, Intel PCI-address-derived names) exists precisely to avoid this.
confirmed.recommendation: Derive device names from stable identity: sanitized PCI address (e.g. gpu-0000-c3-00-0) as the primary key, falling back to a vendor UUID where available. Keep the inventory join (plugin.go) keyed on the same stable identity, and treat a name whose PCI address no longer matches as a prepare error rather than a silent remap.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line. probe.go:150-179 assigns Index as a dense counter (idx++) over lexicographic os.ReadDir of /sys/class/drm|accel; probe.go:78-80 Name()=kind+Index — not PCI address, not UUID, not even the kernel cardN number (card0+card2 with card1 missing yields gpu0/gpu1). publisher.go:141 publishes this name as the ResourceSlice device name; pciAddress exists only as an attribute (publisher.go:79-80), unused as a join key. plugin.go:103-107 joins persisted claim.status.allocation device names to hardware purely by name and errors only when the name is absent — if the name now denotes different silicon, editsFor (plugin.go:137-163) silently injects the wrong /dev nodes. No mitigation exists: probe_test.go:217 only tests two walks of an unchanged tree; scenarios.sh has no rename/reorder coverage. The finding actually understates the exposure: internal/hotplug/hotplug.go triggers immediate re-probe on drm/accel/pci uevents and Inventory.Set (plugin.go:43-51) re-densifies names mid-boot, so a single device removal renames all survivors without any reboot, while allocated claims keep stale names indefinitely. README.md:67's own tenet ('identity lives in attributes, never names') is violated by using names as the durable identity across the allocation lifetime. For the stated scale-up target (heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes, mixed drivers, varying kernels — where DRM enumeration order varies with async probe, module order, simpledrm handoff), silent wrong-device binding of the core allocate→prepare contract with no error path justifies blocker. Recommendation (PCI-address-derived names + prepare-time identity cross-check) is sound and the probe already collects the needed PCIAddr.

confirmed.title: Hardcoded single slice per pool — dense nodes overflow the 128-device (64 with taints) API limit and publish nothing
confirmed.evidence: internal/publisher/publisher.go:183-191 BuildResources: 'Pools: map[...]{nodeName: {Slices: []resourceslice.Slice{{Devices: devices}}}}' — always exactly one slice; publisher_test.go:113-114 asserts len(pool.Slices)==1. Upstream limits in k8s.io/api@v0.35.0/resource/v1/types.go:263-264: ResourceSliceMaxDevices=128, ResourceSliceMaxDevicesWithTaintsOrConsumesCounters=64. The resourceslice helper (dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0/resourceslice/resourceslicecontroller.go) contains no slice-splitting logic — it publishes the slices the driver hands it.
confirmed.impact: A node with >127 accelerator entries (cpu0 counts too) — realistic with SR-IOV virtual functions (Intel Flex: up to 62 VFs per card), future MIG-style partitions, or many NPU/GPU minors — fails apiserver validation on every sync; the helper retries forever and the node publishes NO inventory (or serves a stale slice). With --publish-taints (main.go:39, publisher.go:144-150) the ceiling halves to 64 the moment any device is unhealthy, so enabling the health-taint feature can take a previously-working dense node offline in the scheduler.
confirmed.recommendation: Chunk devices into multiple resourceslice.Slice entries (e.g. 100 per slice, or 60 when taints are enabled) in BuildResources; the helper and the pool generation/resourceSliceCount machinery already support multi-slice pools. Add a test with 200 devices.

verdict.reasoning: Every mechanical claim verified against source. (1) internal/publisher/publisher.go:183-191 hardcodes exactly one resourceslice.Slice per pool; publisher_test.go:113-115 asserts len(pool.Slices)==1. (2) k8s.io/api@v0.35.0/resource/v1/types.go:263-264 confirms ResourceSliceMaxDevices=128 and ResourceSliceMaxDevicesWithTaintsOrConsumesCounters=64. (3) The dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0 resourceslice helper has no splitting logic: validation.go:31-79 checks only duplicate device names and counter refs (never counts), and the controller publishes len(pool.Slices) as resourceSliceCount verbatim (resourceslicecontroller.go:717); grep for MaxDevices in the package returns nothing. (4) Failure mode confirmed: apiserver rejection makes syncPool error, triggering errorHandler + queue.AddRateLimited perpetual retry (resourceslicecontroller.go:571-579), so an over-limit node publishes no (or stale) inventory. (5) Taint halving is real but triple-gated: --publish-taints is off by default (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:39), the taint is only added to unhealthy devices (publisher.go:144-150), and the 64 ceiling only matters with >64 devices. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED major→minor: the probe (internal/probe/probe.go:104-137) enumerates only physical /sys/class/drm cardN + /sys/class/accel accelN entries plus one CPU; the driver has no MIG/partition feature that multiplies counts. For the owner's stated heterogeneous scale-up (mixed-vendor multi-GPU nodes, NPUs, CPU-only nodes) realistic per-node counts are <20 — an order of magnitude under the 128 limit and well under 64. The only path over the limit is SR-IOV VF-dense configs (e.g. Intel Flex 62 VFs/card), which is not in the current design scope and speculative. Real latent ceiling with a catastrophic-when-triggered failure mode and a cheap fix (chunk into ~60-device slices; helper supports multi-slice pools), so worth fixing as future-proofing, but not a major blocker on the stated scale-up path.

confirmed.title: llmfit↔probe GPU pairing is vendor-only — wrong model/VRAM/bandwidth attributes on mixed-model same-vendor nodes
confirmed.evidence: internal/publisher/publisher.go:164-179 matchLLMFitGPU: 'for i := range sys.GPUs { if llmfit.VendorOf(sys.GPUs[i]) == vendor { return &sys.GPUs[i] } }' — returns the FIRST llmfit GPU of the matching vendor for every probed card of that vendor; comment at :161-163 assumes 'llmfit groups identical models', which only holds for homogeneous multi-GPU. No PCI-address or name-based pairing.
confirmed.impact: The most common heterogeneous-node shape — AMD APU iGPU + AMD dGPU (or two different NVIDIA models) — publishes both devices with the attributes of whichever llmfit entry comes first: wrong model name, wrong memory capacity (publisher.go:114-116 memBytes from lf.VRAMGB), wrong bandwidth. Fit CEL (claim.rs:129-132 compares device.capacity memory and memoryBandwidthGBs) then admits models onto devices that physically cannot hold them, or rejects capable ones.
confirmed.recommendation: Pair llmfit GPUs to probed devices by PCI address (extend llmfit's JSON to carry it) or at minimum by model-name/device-ID match; when pairing is ambiguous, fall back to the index/probe source for that device instead of guessing.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all cited code. publisher.go:164-179 matchLLMFitGPU is vendor-only first-match, exactly as quoted; it ignores PCI device ID/address/name of the probed card. llmfit's JSON cannot support better pairing today: GpuInfo (third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/hardware.rs:34-40) has only name/vram_gb/backend/count/unified_memory, and count groups per-model, so mixed-model same-vendor nodes produce multiple distinct entries with VendorOf (internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:76-90) mapping them to the same vendor — every probed card of that vendor receives the FIRST entry's model, backend, unifiedMemory, bandwidth, and memory capacity (publisher.go:97-116, memBytes from lf.VRAMGB at :114-116). Correct per-device sources exist but are shadowed: probe VRAMBytes only used when memBytes==0 (:131-137), index entry only used as source when lf==nil (:117) or for missing bandwidth (:105-110). Downstream, llmfit claim CEL (claim.rs render(): compares device.capacity memory and memoryBandwidthGBs against computed fit bounds) allocates purely on these corrupted values, so models are admitted onto devices that cannot hold them or capable devices are rejected. No mitigation: publisher_test.go:172-258 covers only single-GPU/homogeneous llmfit cases; the live cluster is single-GPU Strix Halo so scenarios cannot catch it; the comment at :161-163 explicitly encodes the homogeneous assumption. Severity major is appropriate for the heterogeneous scale-up goal (AMD iGPU+dGPU and mixed NVIDIA nodes are common target shapes and scheduling correctness is corrupted), but not blocker: homogeneous/single-GPU nodes and device identity/CDI prep (probe-derived) are unaffected. Recommendation (PCI-address in llmfit JSON, or model-name/device-ID pairing with fallback to index/probe on ambiguity) is sound.

confirmed.title: VRAM detection is amdgpu-only — discrete NVIDIA/Intel GPUs advertise memory = whole system RAM when llmfit is unavailable
confirmed.evidence: internal/probe/probe.go:166 'VRAMBytes: readUint(filepath.Join(devDir, "mem_info_vram_total"))' — mem_info_vram_total is an amdgpu sysfs file; nvidia/nouveau/xe don't expose it, so VRAMBytes=0. probe.go:83-85 UnifiedMemory() returns true whenever VRAMBytes==0. publisher.go:117-127 (index/probe fallback paths) then set unifiedMemory=true ('d.UnifiedMemory() || entry.UnifiedMemory', :123) and publisher.go:131-136 sets memBytes = systemRAM. internal/index/index.go:17-24 Entry has Model/MemoryBandwidthGBs/UnifiedMemory but NO VRAM field to correct this.
confirmed.impact: On any NVIDIA/Intel dGPU node where llmfit fails, is disabled (--llmfit-bin=""), or doesn't recognize the card, an 80GB H100 on a 2TB host publishes capacity.memory=2TB and unifiedMemory=true. The generated fit CEL (device.capacity['llmfit.ai'].memory >= NGi) then schedules models far larger than VRAM onto the device — the exact failure the project exists to prevent. The advertised 'degrade to embedded index' path (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:150-160) is therefore unsafe for discrete GPUs.
confirmed.recommendation: Add per-vendor VRAM probes (nvidia: /proc/driver/nvidia or NVML-free sysfs is absent — use the index; xe/i915: lmem sysfs) and a VRAMGB field to index entries; never fall back to systemRAM for devices whose vendor is known to ship discrete VRAM (only do so when unifiedMemory is positively known).

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line. probe.go:166 reads VRAM only from amdgpu's mem_info_vram_total sysfs file (no nvidia/nouveau/i915/xe equivalent probed; grep confirms no other VRAM source in Go code), so discrete NVIDIA/Intel GPUs get VRAMBytes=0. probe.go:83-85 then reports UnifiedMemory()=true. In publisher.go, the index fallback path (:117-123) publishes unifiedMemory=true via 'd.UnifiedMemory() || entry.UnifiedMemory', and :131-136 sets memBytes=systemRAM since VRAMBytes==0. internal/index/index.go:17-25 Entry has no VRAM field, and data.json's H100 entry (10de:2330) carries only model+bandwidth=3350, so the index cannot correct memory but DOES satisfy the bandwidth term of the generated CEL. claim.rs:130-131 confirms fit CEL is memory>=NGi && memoryBandwidthGBs>=bw with no unifiedMemory check — so an 80GB H100 on a large-RAM host would accept models far exceeding VRAM whenever the code is on the fallback path. That path is real and advertised: main.go:149-160 degrades to the index on llmfit error or --llmfit-bin=""; additionally publisher.go matchLLMFitGPU's vendor-name heuristic can leave GPUs unmatched on mixed-vendor nodes even with llmfit working. No test/config mitigates it; the live Strix Halo cluster (unified-memory APU) never exercises this case, which is precisely why it surfaces at heterogeneous scale. Severity major stands: silent capacity over-advertisement causing the exact scheduling failure the project prevents, but only on the degraded path, so not a blocker.

confirmed.title: Positional device names (gpu0/gpu1) are not stable hardware identities; Prepare joins allocation to /dev nodes by name alone — wrong physical GPU can be prepared after enumeration shift
confirmed.evidence: internal/probe/probe.go:78-79 `func (d Device) Name() string { return fmt.Sprintf("%s%d", d.Kind, d.Index) }`; probe.go:149-162 Index is a positional counter (`idx := 0 ... Index: idx ... idx++`) over os.ReadDir of /sys/class/drm — not derived from cardN number or PCI address. internal/publisher/publisher.go:141 publishes `Name: d.Name()` into ResourceSlices, so allocation results carry only this positional name. internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:103 `dev, ok := p.inv.lookup(alloc.Device)` resolves the name against the *current* re-probe snapshot (main.go:144 `inv.Set(probed)` runs every 30s and on uevents via internal/hotplug) with no cross-check of PCI address/vendor against what was allocated.
confirmed.impact: On a multi-GPU node, any event that shifts DRM enumeration between allocation and prepare (hot-detach — which the driver's own hotplug listener is built for — driver unbind/rebind after a GPU reset, kernel upgrade reordering, card numbering gaps like card1+card2 after rebind) silently remaps gpuN to a different physical device. prepareClaim succeeds and injects the wrong GPU's /dev nodes: a claim CEL-selected for the 96GB AMD card can be handed the 8GB Intel card with no error anywhere. Only the lucky case (device fully gone) hits the plugin.go:107 'not in current inventory' error. This defeats the entire llmfit fit-CEL premise on exactly the heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes the owner wants to scale to.
confirmed.recommendation: Make the published device name a stable identity: derive it from PCI address (e.g. gpu-0000-c5-00-0) or at minimum keep Kind+PCIAddr as the inventory key and record PCIVendor/PCIDevice in the name. If names must stay short, have prepareClaim verify the looked-up device's PCI address/vendor against a stable attribute round-tripped through the ResourceSlice pool (bump pool generation on any rename and fail prepare when the name's backing hardware changed).

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line verified. probe.go:150-179: device Index is a dense positional counter over lexically-sorted os.ReadDir of /sys/class/drm, not derived from cardN or PCI address; probe.go:78-80 Name()=kind+index (comment falsely calls it 'stable'). publisher.go:141 publishes this name as the ResourceSlice device name, so DRA allocation results carry only the positional name. plugin.go:103 prepareClaim resolves alloc.Device by name against the current inventory snapshot, which main.go:144 rewrites every probe interval and on hotplug uevents (main.go:102-119); grep confirms no PCI-address/vendor cross-check anywhere in internal/nodeplugin — the only failure mode is the name being wholly absent (plugin.go:107). Concrete unmitigated scenario on a 2-GPU AMD+Intel node: AMD unbind/reset removes card0, next probe renames Intel card1 to gpu0, and a claim CEL-allocated to the AMD device prepares successfully with the Intel card's /dev nodes injected — silent wrong-hardware handoff. Exposure window spans the whole claim lifetime (prepare runs at each pod start), and enumeration shifts (rebind, reboot module-load-order races on mixed-vendor nodes, kernel upgrades) are realistic on the target fleet; the driver's own hotplug listener makes the trigger a supported flow. No mitigation exists: pciAddress is published as an attribute (publisher.go:80) but never round-tripped or verified at prepare; pool-generation bumps protect only new scheduling, not existing allocations; no test covers rename-under-allocation. Single-device-per-kind nodes (like the live kind cluster) are unaffected, but the stated scale-up goal explicitly includes heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes, where silent wrong-device preparation defeats the fit-CEL premise entirely — blocker stands. Fix is cheap since PCIAddr is already probed (probe.go:170).

confirmed.title: No GC for orphaned CDI specs in /var/run/cdi — a crash after writeSpec but before the gRPC response, or kubelet checkpoint loss, orphans llmfit.ai-<uid>.json forever
confirmed.evidence: internal/nodeplugin/cdi.go is the complete on-disk state machinery: only writeSpec (cdi.go:56-81) and removeSpec (cdi.go:86-92) exist; grep across internal/ and cmd/ shows no ReadDir/sweep of cdiDir anywhere (the only ReadDirs are sysfs walks in probe.go:141,208). cdi.go:1-5 explicitly claims 'one file per claim ... doubles as the plugin's on-disk state — restarts need no checkpoint', but nothing ever enumerates that state. Crash point: plugin.go:121 writeSpec succeeds, then the process dies before PrepareResourceClaims returns — kubelet never records the claim as prepared, so NodeUnprepareResources is never issued for that UID and the file is permanent. Same result if /var/lib/kubelet (the kubelet DRA checkpoint) is wiped during node recovery while claims are torn down, or if pod+claim are force-deleted while the DaemonSet is down long enough for kubelet cleanup to give up. cdi.go:68-72's deferred os.Remove covers error returns but not a hard crash between CreateTemp and rename, leaking .llmfit-*.json.tmp files too. hack/scenarios.sh scenario 8 (lines 308-336) tests restart with kubelet-driven unprepare, but no scenario covers deletion-while-down.
confirmed.impact: At fleet scale, /var/run/cdi accumulates stale spec files on crashy or recovered nodes. containerd rescans the dynamic CDI dir on container creation, so hundreds of dead specs add parse/inotify churn; stale specs referencing removed /dev nodes sit in the registry indefinitely; on hosts where /var/run is not cleared (or long-lived kind nodes) this is unbounded. It also falsifies the stated invariant that the CDI file *is* the plugin's state — state with no reconciler.
confirmed.recommendation: On startup (and periodically), list <cdiDir>/llmfit.ai-*.json, extract claim UIDs from filenames, and delete files whose ResourceClaim UID no longer exists (a single filtered LIST of ResourceClaims, or compare against kubelet-known prepared claims). Also sweep *.tmp files older than a few minutes. This is exactly the pattern kubelet expects since UnprepareResourceClaims is documented (draplugin.go:98-110) as possibly never arriving from the preparing instance.

verdict.reasoning: Code facts verified: internal/nodeplugin/cdi.go:56-92 contains only writeSpec/removeSpec; grep confirms no enumeration/sweep of cdiDir anywhere in internal/ or cmd/; cdi.go:3-5 asserts file-is-state with no reconciler; the crash window at plugin.go:121 (writeSpec succeeds, process dies before gRPC response, kubelet never checkpoints the claim, unprepare never issued) is real, as is the .tmp leak on hard crash between cdi.go:68 CreateTemp and cdi.go:80 Rename. So the finding survives as a genuine gap. However, severity 'major' is inflated: (1) the common failure self-heals — if the pod stays pending, kubelet retries prepare and specPath (cdi.go:50-52) is UID-keyed so the retry overwrites the same file and normal unprepare removes it; the leak requires crash in a millisecond window AND pod/claim deletion before any successful retry, or a /var/lib/kubelet checkpoint wipe with /var/run intact; (2) orphaned specs are inert — device IDs embed the claim UID (plugin.go:109), never referenced by any future container, so no correctness impact, only registry clutter; (3) /var/run is tmpfs (verified live: /var/run/cdi on the kind node is empty with a fresh mtime), so 'forever'/'unbounded' is false — accumulation is bounded per boot and realistically a handful of files; (4) .llmfit-*.json.tmp dotfiles are ignored by CDI registries which only parse *.json/*.yaml; (5) the recommendation misreads upstream docs — draplugin.go:97-110 (dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0) says unprepare may come from a different driver instance (handled by the file design and tested in hack/scenarios.sh scenario 8 lines 309-336), not that unprepare may never arrive for a checkpointed prepared claim. A startup sweep of <cdiDir>/llmfit.ai-*.json against live ResourceClaim UIDs is cheap and worth doing at fleet scale, but this is a hygiene gap, not a scale-up blocker.

confirmed.title: HandleError ignores the helper's fatal-vs-recoverable contract — a dead DRA/registrar gRPC server leaves a zombie pod that logs and keeps 'Running'
confirmed.evidence: internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:168-170: `func (p *Plugin) HandleError(...) { klog.FromContext(ctx).Error(err, msg) }` with the comment 'the DaemonSet restart path covers truly wedged sockets'. But the helper (module cache k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0/kubeletplugin/draplugin.go:146-150) defines `ErrRecoverable` to 'distinguish recoverable errors from those errors which are fatal and should cause the process to exit', and only wraps the ResourceSlice-publishing path in recoverableError (draplugin.go:789); the 'DRA gRPC server failed' (draplugin.go:652) and 'registrar gRPC server failed' (draplugin.go:691) calls are NOT recoverable. Meanwhile deploy/daemonset.yaml (lines 27-60) has no livenessProbe or readinessProbe, so there is no 'DaemonSet restart path' at all — nothing restarts a pod whose gRPC server has exited.
confirmed.impact: If the plugin's Serve loop dies (fd exhaustion, socket dir remounted, panic in interceptor), the process keeps running, kubelet deregisters or fails to reach the plugin, and every pod on that node needing an llmfit.ai claim sticks in ContainerCreating while the driver pod reports 1/1 Running. On a big fleet this is a silent per-node outage detectable only by grepping logs.
confirmed.recommendation: In HandleError: `if !errors.Is(err, kubeletplugin.ErrRecoverable) { klog.ErrorS(...); os.Exit(1) }` (or cancel the run context) so kubelet restarts the container; use utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext for the recoverable branch as the helper docs recommend. Add a liveness probe (e.g. touch a healthz file per successful loop iteration, or a small HTTP healthz) to daemonset.yaml.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line verified. plugin.go:168-170 HandleError only logs and never checks kubeletplugin.ErrRecoverable, despite the helper docs (draplugin.go:124-136, v0.35.0) explicitly recommending process exit for fatal errors. The only recoverableError-wrapped path is ResourceSlice publishing (draplugin.go:789); the two gRPC-server-failed calls (652, 691) pass raw fatal errors, and nonblockinggrpcserver.go:80-85 shows the handler fires exactly when Serve exits — after which the server is permanently dead but the process keeps running. main.go:110-134 has no watchdog and returns only on SIGINT/SIGTERM. deploy/daemonset.yaml (HEAD and working tree) has no livenessProbe/readinessProbe, so the comment's 'DaemonSet restart path' does not exist; kubelet does not periodically re-health-check DRA plugins. Refutation attempts (main exits on helper failure, probes exist, helper treats server death as recoverable) all failed against the code. Mitigating factor considered: the trigger is rare (grpc-go retries temporary Accept errors like EMFILE with backoff; handler panics crash the process, which the DaemonSet does restart), so this is a narrow-window latent bug, not a common-path failure. But for the stated goal — many heterogeneous nodes — the failure mode is a silent per-node outage (pod 1/1 Running, all llmfit.ai claims stuck ContainerCreating, detectable only via logs), and the fix is trivial (errors.Is check + os.Exit/context cancel, plus a liveness probe). Severity major stands: contract violation with a comment referencing a nonexistent recovery mechanism, silent at fleet scale.

confirmed.title: Upgrade prepare-unavailability window: RollingUpdate helper option unused, DaemonSet has no updateStrategy/maxSurge, and every restart re-pulls a mutable :main image
confirmed.evidence: internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:75-79 passes only DriverName/KubeClient/NodeName to kubeletplugin.Start — no kubeletplugin.RollingUpdate (draplugin.go:321-355: enables two instances via per-pod-UID sockets + cross-process flock; requires kubelet >=1.33, live cluster is v1.35.0), no FlockDirectoryPath (draplugin.go:445-449: defaults to the plugin data dir, which daemonset.yaml:49-50/68-71 hostPath-mounts at the identical path /var/lib/kubelet/plugins — so file locking would work if enabled). deploy/daemonset.yaml has no spec.updateStrategy (default RollingUpdate maxUnavailable=1, delete-before-create) and lines 29-30 use `image: ghcr.io/sympozium-ai/llmfit-dra:main` + `imagePullPolicy: Always`.
confirmed.impact: Every driver upgrade or pod restart deregisters the plugin for the entire old-pod-teardown + image-pull + re-register window; during it NodePrepareResources fails (new pods on the node stuck ContainerCreating) and NodeUnprepareResources is delayed (terminating pods hang in claim teardown). The window includes a mandatory registry round-trip per restart — a GHCR outage during a node reboot leaves the node without a DRA plugin entirely. Across many nodes the default serial (maxUnavailable=1) rollout is slow, tempting operators to raise maxUnavailable and widen the blast radius. The mutable :main tag also means nodes restarting at different times run different code under one manifest — version skew across a heterogeneous fleet with no rollback identity.
confirmed.recommendation: Set updateStrategy rollingUpdate.maxSurge=1/maxUnavailable=0 and enable kubeletplugin.RollingUpdate(podUID) with the pod UID from the downward API; keep the default Serialize (the flock in the shared plugin data dir then serializes prepare/unprepare across the two overlapping instances — note the current in-memory grpcMutex, draplugin.go:838-840, protects nothing across processes). Pin images by digest or version tag; keep imagePullPolicy Always only for digests.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line verified. internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:75-79 passes only DriverName/KubeClient/NodeName to kubeletplugin.Start; repo-wide grep (excluding third_party) finds no RollingUpdate/POD_UID/updateStrategy/maxSurge anywhere. Upstream k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0 kubeletplugin/draplugin.go confirmed: RollingUpdate(uid) option exists (kubelet>=1.33 required; live cluster is v1.35.0), FlockDirectoryPath defaults to the plugin data dir, and serializeGRPCIfEnabled uses only an in-memory grpcMutex unless rolling updates enable file locking — daemonset.yaml:49-50/68-71 hostPath-mounts /var/lib/kubelet/plugins at the identical path so flock would work. deploy/daemonset.yaml has no spec.updateStrategy; live cluster confirms the default resolves to maxSurge=0/maxUnavailable=1 (delete-before-create), and lines 29-30 are :main + imagePullPolicy Always with a private-registry pull secret (line 18-19), so every container start requires a GHCR round-trip and a pull failure leaves the node with no DRA plugin (ImagePullBackOff, cached image not used under Always). Impact mechanics are correct: old pod's registration socket is gone before the new pod pulls and registers, so NodePrepareResources/NodeUnprepareResources fail for the whole window; :main gives no rollback identity and produces cross-node version skew. Caveats: the live kind cluster actually runs :dev/IfNotPresent (dev-loop deploy differs from the committed manifest), running pods are unaffected and kubelet retries, so this is an availability gap not a correctness bug — not a blocker. The recommendation is directly implementable: main.go:70 already has signal.NotifyContext(SIGINT/SIGTERM), the documented prerequisite for clean rolling-update socket teardown. Major stands for the heterogeneous multi-node scale-up goal.

confirmed.title: DaemonSet not schedulable/durable on the nodes that matter: no tolerations, no priorityClassName
confirmed.evidence: deploy/daemonset.yaml:16-60 — the pod spec contains no `tolerations` and no `priorityClassName`. Requests are 10m/64Mi with only a memory limit (lines 55-60).
confirmed.impact: Heterogeneous GPU clusters routinely taint accelerator nodes (e.g. nvidia.com/gpu:NoSchedule, dedicated=inference:NoSchedule). Without tolerations the driver DaemonSet does not schedule on exactly the nodes whose devices it must publish and prepare — those nodes show zero ResourceSlices and every claim pends. Without system-node-critical priority the driver is evictable under node pressure, taking prepare/unprepare down on loaded nodes.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `priorityClassName: system-node-critical` and broad tolerations (at minimum `operator: Exists` for NoSchedule/NoExecute, as nvidia/dra-example drivers ship), matching the standard device-plugin/DRA DaemonSet pattern.

verdict.reasoning: Verified deploy/daemonset.yaml:16-60 has no tolerations and no priorityClassName; resources at lines 55-60 exactly as cited. grep across deploy/ and hack/ finds no tolerations/priorityClass anywhere; no helm/kustomize overlay exists (Makefile:56 does raw kubectl apply). Live cluster confirms: deployed DS jsonpath for .spec.template.spec.tolerations and .priorityClassName both empty; it only runs today because the sole kind node (kind-control-plane) has zero taints. One nuance weakens but does not kill the finding: the DaemonSet controller auto-injects tolerations for node-condition taints (not-ready, unreachable, memory/disk/pid-pressure, unschedulable), so node-pressure taints alone would not deschedule it. But (a) custom accelerator taints (GKE's automatic nvidia.com/gpu=present:NoSchedule, Karpenter/dedicated-pool taints) are not auto-tolerated — on such nodes the driver never schedules, no ResourceSlices publish, claims pend forever; (b) kubelet eviction-manager eviction is priority-based, so without system-node-critical the Burstable driver pod is evictable under real node pressure, killing prepare/unprepare. Both impact claims hold for the heterogeneous scale-up goal. NVIDIA's DRA driver and dra-example-driver ship system-node-critical + operator:Exists tolerations, so the recommendation is the standard pattern. Severity major is correct (hard blocker on tainted nodes at scale, but trivial manifest fix, not a current-phase blocker).

confirmed.title: NVIDIA proprietary-driver GPUs are schedulable but unpreparable: CUDA device nodes and driver libraries are never injected
confirmed.evidence: internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:137-163 editsFor() injects only d.RenderNode (/dev/dri/renderD*), d.DevNode (/dev/dri/cardN), and '/dev/kfd' when d.Driver == "amdgpu". grep across internal/ shows the only 'nvidia' references are the vendorName PCI-ID mapping (publisher.go:213) and a comment — no /dev/nvidia* path exists anywhere. Meanwhile internal/index/data.json publishes memoryBandwidthGBs for 10de:2684 (RTX 4090), 10de:2b85 (5090), 10de:26b9 (L40S), 10de:2330 (H100 SXM5), so the generated CEL (claim.rs:129-133) WILL select these devices.
confirmed.impact: On any NVIDIA node where nvidia_drm is loaded, the probe sees /sys/class/drm/card0 + renderD128 with driver='nvidia' (the PCI driver symlink), publishes a healthy, bandwidth-attributed device, the scheduler allocates it, prepare succeeds — and the container gets only DRM nodes. CUDA requires /dev/nvidia0, /dev/nvidiactl, /dev/nvidia-uvm (+ /dev/nvidia-uvm-tools, nvidia-caps) plus libcuda/libnvidia-ml from the host (what nvidia-ctk CDI specs mount). None are injected, and CDI edits contain no mounts/hooks at all (cdi.go:34-37 has only Env and DeviceNodes). Every CUDA workload lands and fails at runtime — the worst failure mode: wrong-but-schedulable.
confirmed.recommendation: Either (a) refuse to publish bandwidth/fit attributes for driver=='nvidia' devices (mark them correlation-only, no capacity/bandwidth so fit CEL never selects), or (b) implement real NVIDIA prepare: inject the /dev/nvidia* node set and driver-library mounts (or delegate by referencing the nvidia-ctk-generated CDI spec). Until then, remove the 10de entries from data.json — they weaponize the index against users.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line. editsFor (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:137-163) injects only RenderNode/DevNode DRM nodes plus /dev/kfd gated on Driver=="amdgpu"; repo-wide grep finds zero /dev/nvidia* references. containerEdits (internal/nodeplugin/cdi.go:34-37) supports only Env+DeviceNodes — no mounts/hooks, so libcuda/libnvidia-ml can never be injected with the current schema. internal/index/data.json:27-42 gives 10de:2684/2b85/26b9/2330 memoryBandwidthGBs, and publisher.go:117-123 publishes it with healthy=true (probe.go:64-75: driver bound suffices); the llmfit-generated CEL (claim.rs render(), ~line 129) selects on exactly capacity.memory + memoryBandwidthGBs + healthy, so indexed NVIDIA GPUs are allocatable. Worse than the finding states: mem_info_vram_total is amdgpu-only sysfs, so an NVIDIA dGPU probes VRAMBytes=0 and publisher.go:131-137 falls back to full system RAM as capacity.memory, inflating fit. Attempted refutations that failed: (1) the README's cross-driver matchAttribute-to-gpu.nvidia.com delegation (README.md:52,243; plugin.go:100-101 anticipates mixed claims; publisher.go:88 publishes resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot) is the intended NVIDIA architecture, but it is explicitly 'Blocked on hardware we don't have' and llmfit claim's render() emits a single-request claim against gpu.llmfit.ai with no vendor-driver request and no matchAttribute — the shipped happy path produces the wrong-but-schedulable allocation; (2) scenarios.sh runs on the AMD-only live node and only string-accepts 'nvidia' as a vendor (line 113) — NVIDIA prepare is untested; (3) the repo's own vendorName comment (publisher.go:219-221) treats 'no bandwidth attribute → fit CEL never selects' as the deliberate safety valve for unpreparable virtual adapters, which the 10de index entries bypass for NVIDIA. For the stated scale-up goal (heterogeneous cluster explicitly including NVIDIA), silent schedule-succeeds/prepare-succeeds/runtime-fails on every CUDA workload is a blocker; the finding's recommendation (a) matches the repo's existing pattern.

confirmed.title: Coexistence stance is documentation-only: no defer/refuse on vendor-driver nodes, guaranteeing double-offer with NVIDIA's DRA driver
confirmed.evidence: README (Phase 3 section, ~line 52 and Roadmap line 243) says vendor event streams are 'the vendor DRA driver's job on nodes where one runs' and cross-driver matchAttribute is the alignment mechanism. Code: grep -ri coexist over *.go/*.yaml/*.md returns nothing; plugin.go, publisher.go and main.go contain no detection of vendor DRA drivers, no per-driver opt-out, and deploy/daemonset.yaml (read in full) has no nodeSelector/affinity to exclude vendor-managed nodes — it schedules everywhere. The only implemented artifact is the pcieRoot attribute (publisher.go:83-89), which is opt-in per claim.
confirmed.impact: On a heterogeneous cluster where gpu.nvidia.com (or AMD/Intel DRA drivers) run, the same physical GPU is published twice under two driver names as two independently allocatable devices. The scheduler has no idea they are the same silicon: pod A can claim llmfit.ai/gpu0 while pod B claims the NVIDIA driver's device for the identical card — silent oversubscription of VRAM and compute. matchAttribute only aligns devices within a single claim; it does not prevent cross-claim double allocation.
confirmed.recommendation: Implement the stance: at probe time, detect vendor-managed devices (driver=='nvidia', or presence of another ResourceSlice pool covering the same pciAddress) and either skip publishing them, publish them without capacity/bandwidth (correlation-only), or gate via a per-node/per-vendor allowlist flag. Also document/ship a DaemonSet affinity pattern for clusters running vendor drivers.

verdict.reasoning: Attempted refutation failed; every evidence claim verified against the code. (1) No coexistence mechanism exists: `grep -rin coexist` over *.go/*.yaml/*.md returns nothing; cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:31-39 defines only kubeconfig/node-name/sys-root/proc-root/llmfit-bin/probe-interval/kubelet-plugin/cdi-dir/publish-taints flags — no vendor-skip, allowlist, or correlation-only mode. (2) internal/probe/probe.go:104-140 walks /sys/class/drm and /sys/class/accel unconditionally; internal/publisher/publisher.go maps PCI vendor 10de→"nvidia" (208-213) and publishes every probed GPU as a fully allocatable device with capacity.memory (151-155) — NVIDIA cards are never skipped or downgraded. (3) deploy/daemonset.yaml (read in full, 79 lines) has no nodeSelector/affinity/tolerations gating; it schedules on every node. (4) README.md:50-52 and 243 confirm the design stance is coexistence ("vendor DRA driver's job on nodes where one runs"; roadmap item: live cross-driver matchAttribute against a vendor driver), with resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot (publisher.go:82-89) as the only implemented artifact. (5) The matchAttribute limitation is correct: DRA constraints.matchAttribute aligns devices across requests WITHIN one ResourceClaim; it cannot prevent two independent claims from allocating the same silicon under llmfit.ai and gpu.nvidia.com — the scheduler has no cross-driver identity model. hack/scenarios.sh scenario 11 (lines 433-485) only tests intra-claim gpu+npu alignment between llmfit's own devices, not cross-driver. (6) Aggravating factor: internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:150-165 CDI edits inject only device nodes (/dev/dri, /dev/kfd, /dev/accel) and env vars — no NVIDIA driver-library mounts — so real CUDA workloads on NVIDIA nodes effectively require NVIDIA's stack, pushing operators toward exactly the dual-driver configuration where double-offer occurs.
