Severity corrected from blocker to major: the double-allocation is real and silent (VRAM/compute oversubscription) but conditional — it manifests only when an operator co-deploys a vendor DRA driver on the same node; llmfit-dra as sole allocator (the mode all 12 scenarios and the live Strix Halo cluster exercise) is unaffected, and heterogeneous scale-out across AMD/Intel/NPU/CPU-only nodes works without this bug ever firing. It is not an unconditional blocker to the scale-up, but it does block the README's own advertised cross-driver coexistence story and any NVIDIA node running gpu.nvidia.com, and neither code nor deploy manifests nor docs currently offer any safe-deployment guidance. The recommendation (skip/correlation-only publish for vendor-managed devices, plus a documented DaemonSet affinity pattern) is sound.

confirmed.title: VRAM detection is amdgpu-only: NVIDIA and Intel discrete GPUs publish capacity = whole system RAM and unifiedMemory=true
confirmed.evidence: probe.go:166 reads VRAMBytes exclusively from 'mem_info_vram_total' — an amdgpu sysfs file. i915 exposes lmem_total_bytes, xe exposes tileN/physical_vram_size_bytes, NVIDIA exposes VRAM only via NVML; none are read, so VRAMBytes=0 on every non-AMD dGPU. Then probe.go:83-85 UnifiedMemory() returns true when VRAMBytes==0, publisher.go:123 publishes 'd.UnifiedMemory() || entry.UnifiedMemory' (index path) and publisher.go:131-137 sets memBytes = systemRAM when VRAMBytes==0. data.json has no Intel discrete entries and its NVIDIA entries omit unifiedMemory but can't fix capacity.
confirmed.impact: An Arc A770 (16GB), L40S (48GB) or H100 (80GB) on a 1-2TB-RAM host is published with capacity.memory equal to host RAM and unifiedMemory=true. Fit CEL compares model memory against this fake capacity: a 70B model 'fits' an 8GB card, and unified-memory-aware runtimes are told discrete VRAM is shared RAM. Only the llmfit path (publisher.go:114-116) can rescue this, and only when llmfit's vendor tools (nvidia-smi/rocm-smi) exist inside the container image — they don't, so llmfit degrades to lspci with no vram_gb.
confirmed.recommendation: Add per-driver VRAM probes (i915 lmem_total_bytes, xe tile sizes) and treat 'VRAM unknown' as distinct from 'unified': publish no memory capacity (and let CEL fail closed) rather than defaulting to systemRAM; only default to systemRAM when the platform is genuinely an APU (llmfit- or index-confirmed unifiedMemory).

verdict.reasoning: CORE MECHANISM CONFIRMED: internal/probe/probe.go:166 is the only VRAM probe in the Go driver (grep over internal/+cmd/ finds no lmem_total_bytes, physical_vram_size_bytes, or NVML anywhere) and it reads 'mem_info_vram_total', an amdgpu-only sysfs attribute; i915/xe/nvidia/nouveau never expose it, so VRAMBytes=0 on every non-AMD dGPU. probe.go:83-85 then reports UnifiedMemory()==true for any non-CPU device with VRAMBytes==0, and publisher.go:131-137 defaults capacity.memory to systemRAM. internal/index/index.go:20-24 confirms the index carries only bandwidth+unifiedMemory (no VRAM field), and data.json has zero Intel-discrete entries, so the index cannot fix capacity — exactly as claimed. Live cluster slice confirms the systemRAM-fallback plumbing (npu0 published with capacity=65606144Ki==host MemTotal via the index path). EVIDENCE OVERSTATEMENTS (why not blocker): (1) The claim 'llmfit degrades to lspci with no vram_gb' is wrong for common NVIDIA cards: third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/hardware.rs detect_nvidia_gpu_sysfs_info (lines ~470-550) runs when nvidia-smi is absent, names the card via lspci (image installs pciutils + update-pciids, Dockerfile stage 3) and calls estimate_vram_from_name (hardware.rs:2397+), which knows H100=80, A100=80, L40=48, T4/A10/V100, RTX 20/30/40/50 series, B200 — so an L40S/H100 with DRM nodes present gets approximately-correct capacity via publisher.go:114-116 and unifiedMemory=false via publisher.go:111 (lf.UnifiedMemory=false for discrete). (2) The '70B fits an 8GB card' CEL scenario is gated: llmfit-generated fit CEL (claim.rs:130-131) also requires memoryBandwidthGBs >= N, and probe-only devices carry no bandwidth attribute, so unindexed unknown dGPUs fail the CEL rather than mis-fitting. WHY IT STILL STANDS AS MAJOR FOR THE HETEROGENEOUS GOAL: (a) Intel Arc discrete is definitively broken — llmfit's detect_intel_gpu (hardware.rs:1200-1255) also depends on the amdgpu-only mem_info_vram_total file and otherwise returns Some(0.0) for any lspci 'Arc' match, so publisher.go:131-137 publishes capacity = host RAM for a 16GB A770; no index entry exists to correct it. (b) The rescue path is heuristic string-matching on lspci names — any card missing from estimate_vram_from_name (or a post-image-build GPU) gets capacity=systemRAM. (c) The by-design degradation path (main.go:152-157: llmfit.Detect error → sys=nil → index path) reproduces the finding's worst case exactly: indexed NVIDIA cards (H100/L40S/4090/5090 ARE in data.json with real bandwidth) get unifiedMemory=true (publisher.go:123: d.UnifiedMemory()||entry.UnifiedMemory) plus capacity=systemRAM plus a bandwidth attribute that satisfies the CEL gate — so a 70B model 'fits' a 4090 on a big-RAM host whenever llmfit crashes or its JSON changes shape. (d) ResourceSlices are cluster-wide API data; consumers writing their own memory-only selectors see fabricated capacity regardless of the llmfit CEL's bandwidth gate. The recommendation (per-driver VRAM probes; treat VRAM-unknown as distinct from unified; fail closed instead of defaulting to systemRAM) is architecturally correct — conflating 'no mem_info_vram_total' with 'unified memory' is the root defect and it only works today because the sole test node is a genuine APU. Severity: major is appropriate (not blocker, because the shipped image's llmfit name-estimation does rescue the most common NVIDIA datacenter/consumer SKUs in the happy path; not minor, because Intel discrete is unconditionally wrong and the llmfit-failure fallback silently yields the catastrophic case on indexed NVIDIA hardware).

confirmed.title: llmfit↔probe GPU pairing collapses by vendor: mixed same-vendor multi-GPU nodes get the first GPU's capability applied to all cards
confirmed.evidence: publisher.go:164-179 matchLLMFitGPU: 'for i := range sys.GPUs { if llmfit.VendorOf(sys.GPUs[i]) == vendor { return &sys.GPUs[i] } }' — first same-vendor llmfit entry wins for EVERY probed card of that vendor; there is no PCI-address or name-level join. The len(sys.GPUs)==1 fallback (publisher.go:175-177) pairs a single llmfit GPU with any probed GPU even when vendors disagree. llmfit.go:76-89 VendorOf is a substring heuristic over name+backend.
confirmed.impact: A node with MI300X + RX 7900 XTX (both vendor 'amd') publishes both cards with the MI300X's bandwidth/VRAM/model — the 7900 XTX advertises 5300 GB/s. Same for mixed NVIDIA fleets (H100 + T4). Scheduling decisions based on memoryBandwidthGBs are then wrong on exactly the heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes this scale-up targets. The single-GPU fallback can also glue an llmfit iGPU entry onto a probed virtual framebuffer.
confirmed.recommendation: Join llmfit GPUs to probed devices on PCI address (llmfit would need to emit it) or at minimum on device-ID/model-name match; when multiple same-vendor llmfit entries exist and can't be disambiguated, fall back to the PCI-ID index per card instead of first-match.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against code. (1) matchLLMFitGPU (internal/publisher/publisher.go:164-179) returns the FIRST llmfit GPU whose VendorOf matches the probed card's vendor — no PCI/name/device-ID join; llmfit's JSON (internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:18-25) contains no PCI address, so no finer key exists today. (2) The impact scenario is real: llmfit v0.9.35 emits one GpuInfo per unique model and does not short-circuit across vendors (third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/hardware.rs:136-138, 295-297), and sorts entries VRAM-descending (hardware.rs:285-291), so on a mixed same-vendor node (H100+T4, MI300X+7900XTX) the largest card's model/bandwidth/VRAM is published for EVERY same-vendor probed card via publisher.go:98,104,114-115 — including device memory capacity, which corrupts scheduling. (3) A more common variant strengthens it: llmfit's prefer_discrete_gpus (hardware.rs:283,1115) drops iGPUs, but the probe enumerates all /sys/class/drm cards (internal/probe/probe.go:110), so any AMD APU+dGPU workstation publishes the iGPU with the dGPU's capability. (4) The len(sys.GPUs)==1 fallback (publisher.go:175-177) checks only the llmfit side, so a single Radeon llmfit entry gets glued onto a probed virtio/hyperv framebuffer (vendorName publisher.go:221-226), contradicting the code's own claim (publisher.go:218-220) that virtual adapters never carry bandwidth. (5) No mitigation: publisher_test.go covers only single-llmfit-GPU systems (lines 179, 241); the per-card index fallback (publisher.go:105-109) only fires when the matched llmfit entry lacks bandwidth, so it cannot correct a mismatch; the live single-GPU kind cluster never exercises the path. Severity 'major' is accurate for the heterogeneous multi-GPU scale-up goal — silent wrong attributes on target hardware — but not blocker: identical-model multi-GPU (llmfit count grouping) and mixed-vendor nodes still pair correctly, and the current cluster is unaffected. The recommendation (PCI-address join from llmfit, or model-name/device-ID match, falling back to per-card index) is sound.

confirmed.title: Device names are positional (idx counter over lexically-sorted dir), so gpuN identity is unstable under hotplug/unbind and across reboots on multi-GPU nodes
confirmed.evidence: probe.go:150-179: 'idx := 0 … Index: idx … idx++' assigns names by position in os.ReadDir order (lexical: card0, card1, card10, card2). Names are not derived from the cardN number or PCI address. plugin.go:103-107 joins allocations back to hardware by this name: 'dev, ok := p.inv.lookup(alloc.Device)'. hotplug.go triggers immediate re-probes on unbind/hot-attach, and main.go:144 inv.Set(probed) overwrites the inventory the moment numbering shifts.
confirmed.impact: On a multi-GPU node, if card1's driver unbinds (or a VF/eGPU detaches), the former card2 becomes gpu1 on the next probe. An allocation for 'gpu1' scheduled before the change then prepares a different physical card's /dev nodes — the pod silently gets the wrong GPU (wrong VRAM, possibly wrong vendor). Across reboots, driver load-order races reorder cardN on mixed AMD+NVIDIA nodes, so long-lived allocated claims reference reshuffled hardware. With >9 DRM minors (8-GPU HGX + BMC), lexical sort additionally decouples gpuN from cardN.
confirmed.recommendation: Derive stable device names from PCI address (e.g. hash or normalized BDF, like other DRA drivers do), or at minimum keep Index = the numeric cardN suffix and verify at prepare time that the allocated name's pciAddress attribute still matches the probed device, failing prepare on mismatch.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line checks out. probe.go:150-179 assigns Index from a positional counter over os.ReadDir (lexical) order; the cardN numeric suffix is parsed only as a filter (line 156) and discarded, and Name() (probe.go:78-80) is kind+Index, so gpuN identity shifts whenever an earlier card entry disappears, and decouples from cardN entirely at >9 DRM minors (card10 sorts before card2). plugin.go:103 joins allocations to hardware solely by this name; the 'not in current inventory' guard (104-107) only catches a vanished *name*, not a name remapped to different hardware, so a middle-card removal silently prepares the wrong GPU's /dev nodes. hotplug.go (drm/accel/pci uevents incl. bind/unbind) plus main.go:115-119/144 (inv.Set on every re-probe) make the remap take effect within seconds. No mitigation: publisher.go:80 publishes pciAddress as an attribute but prepare never verifies it (allocation results store only the name); the only stability test (probe_test.go:229) covers unchanged hardware, not removal/reorder; the Inventory doc comment (plugin.go:32-33) explicitly relies on name-as-join-key stability the scheme doesn't provide. On the live 1-GPU node the bug is latent (sole-device removal fails loudly), but for the stated goal — heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes with hotplug explicitly in the driver's design scope — silent wrong-device preparation is a real data-path correctness bug. Major is the right severity (needs a topology change coinciding with a live allocation, so not a blocker, but not minor). The recommendation (PCI-BDF-derived names or pciAddress verification at prepare) is sound and matches upstream DRA driver practice.

confirmed.title: NVIDIA nodes without nvidia_drm loaded publish no GPU at all; RAS/ECC health is amdgpu-only everywhere else
confirmed.evidence: probe.go:107-120 Walk() enumerates GPUs solely via /sys/class/drm. The NVIDIA proprietary stack creates DRM class entries only when the nvidia_drm module is loaded (common on desktops, frequently absent on headless datacenter installs). There is no /sys/bus/pci fallback scan for class 0x0300 devices. Health: probe.go:168 reads 'ras/ue_count' (amdgpu-only); Healthy() (probe.go:64-75) therefore reports healthy=true for any bound non-AMD device regardless of ECC state — README's health table ('no uncorrectable RAS errors') overstates this as general.
confirmed.impact: Two node classes lie differently: (a) headless NVIDIA nodes silently publish only cpu0 — inventory under-reports, and operators see 'no GPU' with no signal why; (b) NVIDIA/Intel GPUs with uncorrectable ECC errors stay healthy=true and allocatable, since NVML/XID-style sources are unimplemented (README calls them hardware-blocked). Combined with the CUDA-prepare blocker, NVIDIA health truth is zero either way.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a /sys/bus/pci/devices scan for display-class devices with no DRM entry (publish as correlation-only/unpreparable, healthReason='noDrmNode'), and scope the health claim in README/attributes to amdgpu until vendor sources exist.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all three legs of the finding against the code; none refuted. (1) Enumeration: internal/probe/probe.go:110 — Walk() enumerates GPUs exclusively via /sys/class/drm (walkClass on 'card' prefix); grep confirms zero /sys/bus/pci/devices class-0x0300 scanning anywhere in the repo. The kernel premise is correct: the NVIDIA proprietary stack creates DRM class entries only via nvidia_drm.ko; nvidia.ko+nvidia_uvm alone (common headless/NVML-only setups) yield no cardN entry. (2) I checked the two plausible mitigations and both fail: (a) llmfit's nvidia-smi/lspci fallback (README line 58, internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:3-4) cannot add devices — publisher.BuildDevices (internal/publisher/publisher.go:40-42) iterates only over probed devices; llmfit System output is used solely as enrichment via matchLLMFitGPU (publisher.go:92,164-176). A GPU seen by nvidia-smi but absent from /sys/class/drm publishes nothing. (b) The hotplug listener (internal/hotplug/hotplug.go:25) subscribes to pci uevents but only re-triggers the same DRM-only Walk(). Note the driver explicitly intends to publish NVIDIA devices (vendor '10de'→'nvidia' at publisher.go:212-213, README attribute table line 72), and the Phase-3 cross-driver matchAttribute story depends on publishing resource.kubernetes.io/pcieRoot for the NVIDIA device (publisher.go:88) — which is impossible if the device is never enumerated, so the gap also breaks the project's own vendor-alignment design, not just standalone inventory. (3) Health: probe.go:168 reads device/ras/ue_count, an amdgpu-only sysfs node (readUint returns 0 on absence, probe.go:236-246), so Healthy() (probe.go:64-75) returns true for any bound Intel/NVIDIA GPU regardless of ECC state; the code comment at probe.go:55 even admits '0 where the driver doesn't expose it'. README line 82 states the health semantic generically ('kernel driver bound, no uncorrectable RAS errors') without amdgpu scoping, though README line 243 does honestly flag XID/DCGM as hardware-blocked — so 'overstates' is fair for the attribute table, partially mitigated in the roadmap text. Severity: major is appropriate for the stated heterogeneous scale-up — an entire node class (headless NVIDIA) can silently under-report inventory with no healthReason/log signal distinguishing 'no GPU' from 'GPU present but no DRM node', and the recommendation (PCI display-class fallback published as correlation-only + scoping the health claim) is proportionate. Not a blocker because NVIDIA nodes typically also run NVIDIA's own DRA driver for actual allocation, and llmfit-dra's NVIDIA prepare path is separately blocked anyway.

confirmed.title: Generated fit CEL hard-errors on devices missing memoryBandwidthGBs, excluding whole nodes from allocation
confirmed.evidence: third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/claim.rs:129-132 generates unguarded `device.attributes['llmfit.ai'].memoryBandwidthGBs >= {bw}`. internal/publisher/publisher.go:124-127 (probe fallback) publishes kind=gpu devices with NO memoryBandwidthGBs attribute for any unindexed SKU, and publisher.go:218-227 explicitly publishes virtual display adapters (microsoft/vmware/virtio — and any ASPEED BMC framebuffer bound by `ast` lands here too via probe.go:140-179) claiming 'they carry no bandwidth attribute, so fit CEL never selects them'. That claim is wrong: missing map keys are CEL runtime errors ('no such key', dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0/cel/compile_test.go:74), and the allocator aborts the entire node's allocation on a selector runtime error instead of skipping the device (structured/internal/stable/allocator_stable.go:986-992 returns error; :854-857 propagates out of the device loop). The BMC/virtual card even passes the memory conjunct because publisher.go:131-137 assigns it full systemRAM capacity.
confirmed.impact: On a heterogeneous fleet, any node with one unindexed or virtual kind=gpu/npu device — every rack server with a BMC VGA (typically card0 → gpu0, evaluated first), every VM with virtio-gpu, every GPU SKU newer than the embedded index — fails Filter with a CEL runtime error for every fit-generated claim, even if the node also has 8 perfectly matching GPUs. Worst case: fit claims are unschedulable cluster-wide.
confirmed.recommendation: Always publish memoryBandwidthGBs (defaulting to 0) for gpu/npu kinds in BuildDevices, and/or make `llmfit claim` emit a guarded expression: `'memoryBandwidthGBs' in device.attributes['llmfit.ai'] && ...`. Add a scenario with a probe-only device plus a fit claim to catch this class of error.

verdict.reasoning: Attempted refutation failed; every cited claim verified against code, and I reproduced the failure empirically with live cluster data. (1) Unguarded CEL confirmed: third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/claim.rs render() (~lines 129-137) emits `device.attributes['llmfit.ai'].memoryBandwidthGBs >= {bw}` with no `in`-guard; verified by running the prebuilt binary: `llmfit claim Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B --min-tps 20` emits exactly that, with deviceClassName defaulting to `llmfit.ai` (ClaimTarget::default, claim.rs:40) — the class that matches ALL kinds including cpu0 (deploy/deviceclass.yaml: `device.driver == "llmfit.ai"`). (2) Devices without the attribute confirmed: internal/publisher/publisher.go:124-127 (probe fallback for unindexed GPUs/NPUs) and — worse than the finding states — the KindCPU branch (publisher.go:54-72) NEVER sets memoryBandwidthGBs, so every node's cpu0 lacks it; live cluster confirms (kubectl: cpu0 attrs = healthy/kind/model/source/unifiedMemory/vendor, no bandwidth). probe.go:110-114,140-179 confirmed: any /sys/class/drm/cardN becomes kind=gpu (ASPEED 1a03 isn't even in vendorName's virtual list, publisher.go:208-230, so BMC framebuffers hit the probe fallback). publisher.go:131-137 confirmed: such devices get capacity.memory = systemRAM, so the memory conjunct passes. (3) CEL/allocator behavior confirmed empirically: I ran k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation@v0.35.0 (repo's go.mod version; live cluster is v1.35.0) in a temp dir — DeviceMatches returns `no such key: memoryBandwidthGBs` when the memory conjunct passes (error absorbed by && only when another conjunct is false), and allocator_stable.go:986-991 wraps it as 'CEL runtime error' with :854-857 propagating out of the device loop, aborting the whole node. (4) Decisive end-to-end repro: I fed the LIVE node's actual ResourceSlice (kind-control-plane: cpu0/gpu0/npu0) plus the generated-claim shape into structured.NewAllocator().Allocate() — result: zero allocations, err='claim default/qwen-qwen2-5-7b-fit: selector #0: CEL runtime error: no such key: memoryBandwidthGBs', even though gpu0 fully satisfies the fit (bw=256 >= 161, healthy, memory OK). cpu0 sorts first (probe.go:128-133: cpu < gpu < npu) and its memory (62Gi >= 5Gi floor) passes, so the error is not absorbed. This means default-class fit claims fail on essentially EVERY node today (any node whose RAM >= the model's memory floor), not only heterogeneous fleets — hack/scenarios.sh scenario 9 should currently fail on the live node; CI never catches it because GitHub runners have no fit-capable gpu0 so HAS_GPU=0 skips scenario 9 (scenarios.sh:104-122), i.e. 'CI is green' provides no coverage of this path. The comment at publisher.go:218-220 ('they carry no bandwidth attribute, so fit CEL never selects them') encodes the wrong mental model — missing attributes error, they don't fail-closed. No existing mitigation found (no guard, no default attribute, no scenario). Severity 'blocker' is correct for the scale-up goal; the recommendation (always publish memoryBandwidthGBs for gpu/npu — and note cpu0 needs it too or the claim needs a guard/kind-scoped class — plus a probe-only-device + fit-claim scenario) is sound.

confirmed.title: Driver DaemonSet has no priorityClassName: evictable under node pressure, wedging claim lifecycle while slices stay published
confirmed.evidence: deploy/daemonset.yaml:16-60 — the pod spec has no priorityClassName (confirmed empty on the live DS via jsonpath) and is Burstable (requests cpu:10m/memory:64Mi, limits memory:256Mi, lines 55-60). kubelet eviction ranks by priority then usage-over-requests, and llmfit exec every 30s (main.go:152-159) pushes usage past the 64Mi request. Comparable drivers (NVIDIA device plugin, dra-example-driver) run system-node-critical.
confirmed.impact: Under memory pressure — most likely on busy GPU nodes, exactly where the driver matters — the driver is evicted before workload pods. While down: the kubelet plugin socket is gone so NodePrepareResources fails (new claim pods stuck ContainerCreating) and NodeUnprepareResources fails (deleting pods stuck Terminating, claims never freed); meanwhile ResourceSlices are independent API objects that remain published (publisher.go:183-206), so the scheduler keeps allocating devices onto the dead-driver node, compounding the pileup. Under sustained pressure the DS controller recreate loop flaps.
confirmed.recommendation: Set priorityClassName: system-node-critical, make the pod Guaranteed QoS (requests == limits sized from real llmfit usage), and consider marking published devices or a readiness signal tied to plugin socket health.

verdict.reasoning: Verified on both manifest and live cluster: deploy/daemonset.yaml:16-60 has no priorityClassName and Burstable resources (requests 10m/64Mi, limits mem 256Mi only, lines 55-60); live DS pod has priority=0, qosClass=Burstable (kubectl jsonpath). Uncommitted working-tree changes do not add priority. main.go:36 + daemonset.yaml:32 confirm 30s probe cadence exec-ing llmfit, whose RSS counts against the 64Mi request (could not measure — no metrics-server in kind). Eviction mechanics hold: priority-0 Burstable node agent ranks below system-critical pods; DS controller auto-tolerates memory-pressure taints so the recreate/evict flap loop is real. Impact on claim lifecycle is real: plugin socket gone → NodePrepare/UnprepareResources fail, pods stuck ContainerCreating/Terminating. NVIDIA DRA driver and dra-example-driver both default to system-node-critical, so the comparison is accurate. One sub-claim is overstated: ResourceSlices are Node-owned (publisher.go:193-206, confirmed live) but the cluster is v1.35, where kubelet wipes a deregistered driver's slices ~30s after the plugin socket is removed, and main.go:70/93 handles SIGTERM with defer helper.Stop() — so under graceful eviction the scheduler stops allocating after ~30s. Under hard eviction (0s grace) or OOM-kill the deferred cleanup never runs, the stale socket remains on hostPath, and the stale-slices/compounding-pileup scenario survives. Net: finding stands; major is appropriate for the heterogeneous busy-GPU-node scale-up goal since memory pressure is most likely exactly where the driver is most needed, and the fix (system-node-critical + Guaranteed QoS) is the established convention.

confirmed.title: No tolerations: driver never lands on tainted accelerator nodes
confirmed.evidence: deploy/daemonset.yaml:16-60 contains no tolerations block (live DS jsonpath for .spec.template.spec.tolerations is empty). GPU node pools are conventionally tainted (e.g. nvidia.com/gpu:NoSchedule, or cloud-provider accelerator taints); the DS controller only auto-adds node.kubernetes.io/* condition tolerations.
confirmed.impact: On a real heterogeneous cluster with tainted GPU/NPU pools, the driver pod is never scheduled onto exactly the nodes it exists to inventory: no ResourceSlices, no claims schedulable there. Works on kind only because kind strips the control-plane taint.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `tolerations: [{operator: Exists}]` (standard for node-agent DaemonSets) or at minimum tolerate the accelerator taints used by the fleet.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly. deploy/daemonset.yaml (pod spec lines 16-79) has no tolerations, nodeSelector, or affinity; a case-insensitive whole-repo grep for 'toleration' returns zero hits, and deploy/ contains only daemonset.yaml, deviceclass.yaml, rbac.yaml — no chart/overlay/script that could inject tolerations. Live cluster confirms: llmfit-dra DS tolerations field is empty while every other node-agent DaemonSet in the same cluster (kindnet, kube-proxy, and the owner's own sympozium-node-probe and sympozium-llmfit-daemon) carries [{operator: Exists}], showing the project's own ecosystem convention is violated. The single kind node has Taints: <none>, which is exactly why the gap is masked today. For the heterogeneous scale-up goal the impact is real: GPU pools are conventionally tainted (nvidia.com/gpu:NoSchedule via GPU Operator; GKE/EKS/AKS accelerator taints), the DS controller only auto-tolerates node.kubernetes.io/* condition taints, so the driver would silently never schedule on tainted accelerator nodes — no ResourceSlices, no DRA claims schedulable there. Major (not blocker) is right: one-line fix, and only bites fleets that taint accelerator pools, but the failure mode is silent and hits exactly the nodes the driver exists for.

confirmed.title: uevent storm turns the probe loop into a continuous llmfit-exec loop; pci subsystem matches all PCI devices; leading-edge-only debounce publishes half-initialized state
confirmed.evidence: internal/hotplug/hotplug.go:52-54 treats every event on drm/accel/pci as relevant — pci includes NVMe, NIC/SR-IOV VF bind/unbind churn. hotplug.go:94-98 is a leading-edge rate limiter (`if time.Since(last) < debounce { continue }`), not a settle-then-fire debounce. main.go:115-119 makes every signal run desiredState, which unconditionally execs llmfit (main.go:152-159, 30s timeout at internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:44) plus a full sysfs walk and a controller.Update.
confirmed.impact: A misbehaving device (AER error storm, GPU falling off the bus, SR-IOV VF churn) drives up to one full probe+subprocess cycle per 2s per node, indefinitely — back-to-back if llmfit is slow (nvidia-smi without persistence mode takes seconds and touches the wedged GPU driver, potentially worsening it). Additionally, because the probe fires at burst start, it can capture a half-initialized device (driver not yet bound → healthy=false 'driverUnbound', VRAM not yet populated) and publish it as a real slice update — an actual API write per flap, amplified across the fleet, with correction deferred up to 30s to the next tick.
confirmed.recommendation: Debounce trailing-edge (fire after N quiet seconds), filter pci events to accelerator classes/devpaths, add exponential backoff on repeated triggers, and skip the llmfit exec when the sysfs walk result is unchanged from the previous cycle.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all three sub-claims directly in code. (1) internal/hotplug/hotplug.go:22-26 puts "pci" in the subsystems map and relevant() (52-54) checks subsystem only; parseUevent (37-49) does not even extract DEVPATH or PCI class, so NVMe/NIC/SR-IOV VF add/remove/bind/unbind events on any PCI device trigger re-probes — hotplug_test.go:35 confirms subsystem-only matching, no filtering exists. (2) hotplug.go:94-98 is confirmed leading-edge: the first burst event sends the signal and events within the 2s window are dropped without sending or extending, so a driver-bind uevent that follows device-add within 2s produces no signal. A probe fired at burst start can observe Driver=="" -> probe.go:65-70 returns (false,"driverUnbound"), published as the healthy attribute via publisher.go:43-46; correction then waits for the ticker, and deploy/daemonset.yaml:32 sets --probe-interval=30s, exactly matching the claimed up-to-30s staleness. (3) cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:115-119 runs desiredState on every signal; main.go:152-159 unconditionally execs llmfit (llmfit.go:44, 30s timeout) plus a full sysfs walk; the cap-1 channel (hotplug.go:100-103) folds signals arriving mid-probe so a slow llmfit run is immediately followed by another — sustained pci churn yields one exec per ~2s or back-to-back, indefinitely. Partial existing mitigation: an unchanged controller.Update issues no API writes (main.go:128-133), so the API-server impact is limited to genuine state flips (still one real write per flap plus a correction write), but nothing mitigates the local subprocess/vendor-tool churn against a possibly-wedged driver. Minor weakness in the finding: the AER-storm example is speculative (per-error AER events don't reliably emit pci uevents), but VF churn, NVMe hotplug, and GPU fall-off-bus remove/add do, so the mechanism stands. Severity major is appropriate for the heterogeneous multi-node goal: not a blocker (ticker floor bounds staleness, steady state correct, no-op syncs are write-free) but a real per-node runaway-work and transient-wrong-health mode on churn-prone nodes. Recommendation (trailing-edge debounce, devpath/class filter, skip exec on unchanged walk) is sound.

confirmed.title: Hotplug listener dies permanently and silently on netlink read error (ENOBUFS during storms)
confirmed.evidence: internal/hotplug/hotplug.go:81-89: any unix.Read error other than shutdown logs 'uevent read failed; hotplug listener stopping' and returns — the goroutine exits and the events channel simply never fires again. The socket is bound to group 1 receiving ALL kernel uevents with userspace filtering (hotplug.go:62-70), no SO_RCVBUF sizing, 4096-byte buffer (hotplug.go:79); netlink overruns on busy systems classically surface as ENOBUFS.
confirmed.impact: On busy nodes (USB/block/net uevent traffic, or the very device-error storms the listener exists to catch), the kernel socket overruns, the read errors once, and the node silently loses event-driven health/hotplug detection for the life of the pod — degrading to 30s-ticker probing with no metric, restart, or crash to surface it. At 500 nodes this rots invisibly.
confirmed.recommendation: Treat ENOBUFS as recoverable (drain and continue, or close/reopen the socket with backoff), raise SO_RCVBUF, and export a liveness signal/metric for the listener state instead of only a log line.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line. internal/hotplug/hotplug.go:82-88: any non-shutdown unix.Read error logs once and returns; the goroutine dies permanently and main.go:115's <-uevents arm never fires again (channel never closed, no restart/metric/crash). hotplug.go:67 binds Groups=1 receiving ALL kernel uevents (usb/block/net included) with only userspace filtering (line 91), and there is no SO_RCVBUF/SO_RCVBUFFORCE or NETLINK_NO_ENOBUFS anywhere, so per netlink(7) a rcvbuf overrun (default ~208KB) surfaces as ENOBUFS on read — the classic udev-listener failure udevd mitigates with a 128MB forced rcvbuf. The 4096-byte buffer claim is technically moot (netlink truncates without error) but the ENOBUFS-kills-listener mechanism is real, and plausible during coldplug replays, SR-IOV/USB storms, or the GPU error storms the listener exists to catch. Tests (hotplug_test.go) cover parsing only; no mitigation exists for mid-life death. HOWEVER severity is inflated: the design explicitly supports ticker-only operation as the reconciliation floor (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:97-106; startup failure of Listen already degrades to ticker-only), deployed probe-interval is 30s (deploy/daemonset.yaml:32), and main.go:128-133 pushes desired state unconditionally each tick, so worst case is detection latency degrading from ~2s to <=30s with full self-healing — no incorrect or unboundedly stale inventory, no scheduling correctness loss. Also no CPU limit on the container (daemonset.yaml:55-60), reducing throttle-induced reader stalls. Real, cheap-to-fix robustness/observability gap (ENOBUFS should drain-and-continue or reopen with backoff; listener liveness should be surfaced), and at 500 nodes the silence is a legitimate concern, but bounded graceful degradation to an explicitly supported mode makes this minor, not major, for the heterogeneous scale-up goal.

confirmed.title: Device names are enumeration-counter based, not stable: hot-remove silently renames devices under live allocations
confirmed.evidence: internal/probe/probe.go:150-179: Index is a running counter (`idx++`) over lexically-sorted class entries, not derived from the cardN/accelN numeric suffix or PCI address; Device.Name() is kind+Index (probe.go:78-80). internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:103-116 joins allocation results to /dev nodes purely by this name via the latest probe snapshot (inv.Set at main.go:144).
confirmed.impact: On a multi-GPU node, hot-removing card0 makes card1 become 'gpu0' at the next probe: the slice loses gpu1 (breaking pending allocations for it), while a claim still holding 'gpu0' now resolves — at Prepare time — to a different physical card's /dev/dri nodes. Wrong-device injection or spurious 'not in current inventory' failures, exactly on the multi-GPU + hotplug configurations the scale-out targets. (Also >9 GPUs enumerate card10 before card2 lexically, making names diverge from kernel minors.)
confirmed.recommendation: Derive device names from stable identity — PCI address (already probed, probe.go:170) or the sysfs cardN suffix — instead of a walk counter, and treat renames as remove+add.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every citation. probe.go:150-179: device Index is a running counter (idx++) over lexically-sorted /sys/class entries, ignoring the cardN/accelN suffix and PCI address; Name()=kind+Index (probe.go:78-80). publisher.go:141 publishes this name as the ResourceSlice device name (PCIAddr is only an attribute, publisher.go:79-80). nodeplugin/plugin.go:103-116 resolves allocation results to /dev nodes solely via inv.lookup(alloc.Device), and the inventory map is rekeyed by Name() on every re-probe (main.go:144, plugin.go:43-51), including uevent-triggered hotplug re-probes (main.go:102-119, internal/hotplug). Consequence chain holds: hot-removing card0 on a 2-GPU node makes card1 re-enumerate as gpu0 (its DevNode correctly stays /dev/dri/card1), so an existing allocation for gpu0 prepares against the wrong physical card (silent wrong-device CDI injection), and an allocation for gpu1 fails at plugin.go:107 ("not in current inventory") despite the hardware being present. Allocated claims are not revoked when slices change, so the stale-name window is real. No mitigation exists: no test or hack/scenarios.sh scenario covers renumbering; DRM minors are also non-contiguous after unbind/rebind, so divergence needs fewer devices than the >9-GPU lexical point suggests. Severity: major is correct — unaffected on single-GPU-per-kind nodes (like the current kind cluster) and requires a remove/unbind between allocation and prepare, but the multi-GPU + hotplug configuration is exactly the stated scale-out target and the driver's own uevent re-probe makes the rename prompt. Recommendation (name from PCI address or sysfs suffix) is sound; PCIAddr is already probed at probe.go:170.

confirmed.title: matchLLMFitGPU collapses different same-vendor GPUs to the first llmfit entry: wrong capabilities on mixed-model nodes
confirmed.evidence: internal/publisher/publisher.go:164-179: for each probed GPU the loop returns the FIRST llmfit GPU whose VendorOf matches the vendor string; llmfit only groups identical models (count>1). A node with e.g. an MI300X and an embedded Radeon iGPU (both vendor 'amd') gets both probed cards matched to whichever AMD entry llmfit lists first, publishing that entry's name/bandwidth/VRAM for both devices (publisher.go:96-116).
confirmed.impact: On heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes — the stated scale-out target — one of the two cards is published with the other card's memory capacity and bandwidth, so fit CEL selects devices that cannot actually run the model (or rejects ones that could), and Prepare hands the workload the wrong physical device's advertised physics.
confirmed.recommendation: Correlate llmfit GPUs to probed devices by PCI address (extend llmfit's JSON to include bus IDs) or at minimum by model-name and count matching; refuse to publish llmfit-sourced attributes when the pairing is ambiguous and fall back to the index per device.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against code. publisher.go:164-179 matchLLMFitGPU returns the FIRST sys.GPUs entry whose VendorOf(name+backend substring heuristic, llmfit.go:76-90) matches the probed vendor; no model/count/PCI correlation. llmfit upstream (third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/hardware.rs:38, 349-408) groups only identical models into count>1 and emits distinct entries for mixed same-vendor models (its own comment cites 'RX 7600 + R9700'), sorted VRAM-descending. So on a mixed AMD node every AMD card is published with the largest-VRAM entry's name/backend/bandwidth/unifiedMemory, and publisher.go:114-115 sets capacity.memory from that entry's vram_gb, suppressing the probe's per-device VRAMBytes fallback (lines 131-137). Aggravator: a large-RAM APU's unified pool (~62GB) sorts above a 16-24GB dGPU, so the dGPU inherits unifiedMemory=true and the shared-pool size. No mitigation exists: the GPU.Count field is parsed but never consumed; publisher_test.go only tests single-llmfit-entry nodes (lines 172-262); the single-GPU kind cluster cannot exhibit the bug so live scenarios pass. Severity major is correct (not blocker: homogeneous and same-model multi-GPU nodes are handled correctly; failure mode is wrong published capacity/bandwidth causing fit CEL to select unfit devices, which directly hits the stated heterogeneous multi-GPU scale target). Recommendation is sound: correlate by PCI bus ID or model+count, else fall back to the index path already present at publisher.go:117-123.

confirmed.title: Zero multi-node evidence; the suite is structurally single-node and parts become tautological on multi-node clusters
confirmed.evidence: Live cluster is one node (kubectl get nodes: only kind-control-plane; one llmfit.ai slice). CI kind cluster is single-node (.github/workflows/build.yml:51-57, helm/kind-action default). scenarios.sh hardcodes slice [0] everywhere: our_slice | jq '.[0].spec.nodeName' (scenarios.sh:92), '.[0].spec.devices' (line 97), '.[0].metadata.name' (line 136). Scenario 3's recreation check is `[ new_count -ge 1 ] && [ new_name != slice_name ]` (scenarios.sh:139-145) — with 2+ nodes another node's slice satisfies both conditions immediately even if reconciliation is broken. driver_exec picks an arbitrary Running driver pod: `kubectl -n $NS get pod ... | head -1` (scenarios.sh:43-47), so scenarios 7/8 would inspect /var/run/cdi on the wrong node once >1 node exists.
confirmed.impact: No evidence the scheduler picks the right NODE across a fleet (the core value proposition of publishing fit attributes), and the suite cannot even be rerun truthfully on a multi-node cluster: scenario 3 passes vacuously, scenarios 7/8 flake depending on which driver pod head -1 returns. Scenario 1 asserts count>=1, never count==node-count, so a driver that publishes on only one of N nodes still passes.
confirmed.recommendation: Make the suite node-aware: select slices/driver pods by the consumer pod's spec.nodeName; assert slice count == schedulable node count; add a multi-node kind config (kind supports N workers cheaply) with per-node --sys-root fakes to force different inventories, then assert a bandwidth-CEL claim lands on the correct node.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited fact verified. Live cluster: kubectl get nodes shows only kind-control-plane with one llmfit.ai slice. CI: .github/workflows/build.yml uses helm/kind-action@v1 with only node_image/cluster_name (no config:), so default single-node; no kind config file exists in the repo. hack/scenarios.sh hardcodes slice [0] at lines 92, 97, 136 as claimed, PLUS lines 227/230 (scenario 5) and 435 (scenario 11 NPU gate) which the finding missed. Scenario 3 (lines 136-145) passes if new_count>=1 && new_name!=slice_name — with 2+ nodes the other node's slice becomes [0] instantly, so it passes vacuously even with reconciliation broken. driver_exec (lines 44-46) picks `head -1` of Running driver pods; scenarios 7/8 assert /var/run/cdi/llmfit.ai-$uid.json presence via it, uncorrelated with the consumer pod's node, so they flake on multi-node (scenario 12's uevent injection at line 498 shares the defect). Scenario 1 only asserts count>=1 (lines 88-91), never count==node-count. No mitigation exists: internal/publisher/publisher_test.go only tests per-node slice construction; nothing anywhere asserts a consumer pod's spec.nodeName matches the node whose attributes satisfied the CEL (scenario 4 verifies allocated device name only, which on one node proves nothing about node selection). Severity not inflated: the stated next phase is heterogeneous multi-node, whose core value proposition (scheduler picks the right NODE via fit attributes) has zero evidence, and the suite cannot truthfully be rerun on a multi-node cluster to obtain it — vacuous passes plus node-lottery flakes would produce misleading green runs. Recommendation is actionable and matches code structure.

confirmed.title: No scenario ever runs real device compute — assertions stop at env vars and /dev node existence in a busybox container
confirmed.evidence: Consumer image is docker.io/library/busybox:1.36 in every scenario (scenarios.sh:73,192,357,404,466). GPU assertions are `echo $LLMFIT_DEVICE` / `echo $LLMFIT_RENDER_NODE` and `test -e $render` (scenarios.sh:215-221), `test -e /dev/accel/accel0` (line 483). No vulkaninfo/rocminfo/llama.cpp/clinfo is ever invoked. Even /dev/kfd injection for amdgpu (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:159-161) is asserted only in a unit test (plugin_test.go:108), never in a container.
confirmed.impact: The suite proves CDI edits are applied, not that the device is usable. This is exactly the class of check that would green-light an NVIDIA node: editsFor injects only /dev/dri/* nodes (plugin.go:145-158) and never /dev/nvidia0, /dev/nvidiactl, /dev/nvidia-uvm — CUDA workloads would be broken, yet `test -e $LLMFIT_RENDER_NODE` would still pass. Permissions/cgroup device access, ROCm's kfd requirement, and non-root container access are all unverified.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a compute smoke stage per vendor: vulkaninfo --summary (AMD/Intel), a tiny llama.cpp token-generation run against the render node, /dev/kfd open test for ROCm; run it on real hardware in the live suite and gate 'heterogeneous validated' on at least one Intel and one NVIDIA node passing it.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line verified. All 6 consumer pods in hack/scenarios.sh use busybox:1.36 (lines 73,192,271,357,404,467); assertions are env-var echoes and `test -e` on device paths (scenarios.sh ~215-221, 483); grep confirms zero occurrences of vulkaninfo/rocminfo/clinfo/llama/kfd in hack/. /dev/kfd injection (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:159-161) is asserted only in the unit test plugin_test.go:107-108, never live — despite the live node being amdgpu where an in-container kfd/Vulkan smoke is feasible today. The NVIDIA green-light claim is architecturally sound: probe.go:173-174 discovers only /dev/dri/card*/renderD* nodes, editsFor (plugin.go:137-163) adds /dev/kfd only for amdgpu and has no /dev/nvidia0|nvidiactl|nvidia-uvm path, yet NVIDIA is treated as a supported vendor (publisher.go:213, llmfit.go:79-80); CUDA requires /dev/nvidia* not the DRM render node, so `test -e $LLMFIT_RENDER_NODE` passes while workloads are broken. Mitigation check: the driver pod does exec the real llmfit binary (scenario 5, internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:47), but that validates privileged host-side detection, not consumer-container device usability (cgroup device allowlist, permissions, non-root). Severity corrected blocker→major: this is a test-coverage gap whose worst consequence is masking a separate code bug (the NVIDIA node-injection gap is its own finding); the live rig has no Intel/NVIDIA hardware so vendor smoke tests need new infrastructure, but the feasible-today AMD compute smoke is genuinely missing and the suite is the only gate for the heterogeneous claim.

confirmed.title: GPU scenarios have only ever executed on one AMD APU; Intel/NVIDIA GPU and multi-GPU paths have zero end-to-end coverage
confirmed.evidence: CI e2e is GPU-less: scenarios 9/10/11 skip without gpu0/npu0 (scenarios.sh:341,373-374,429-430,487) and build.yml:31-33 documents 'GPU-less kind cluster ... skips its GPU-specific assertions'. The only accelerator hardware ever tested is the single Strix Halo node (amdgpu gpu0 + XDNA npu0, confirmed live). NVIDIA/Intel coverage exists only as static fixtures: publisher_test.go:25 fabricates a probe.Device{PCIVendor:"10de", Driver:"nvidia"} and asserts an index map lookup (publisher_test.go:66-72); probe_test.go fakes an xe iGPU. hack/scenarios-cpu.sh only hides sysfs to collapse inventory to cpu0 (scenarios-cpu.sh:37-39) — it removes heterogeneity rather than adding it, and is not run in CI at all (build.yml runs only ./hack/scenarios.sh, line 66).
confirmed.impact: The 'mixed AMD/Intel/NVIDIA' claim rests on an embedded JSON lookup being unit-tested. Real NVIDIA nodes differ structurally (proprietary driver exposes DRM only with nvidia-drm modeset; device nodes are /dev/nvidia*), and nothing in the test story would catch that the driver publishes a plausible slice yet prepares unusable containers.
confirmed.recommendation: Before claiming heterogeneous readiness, run the live suite (plus the compute smoke test) on at least one discrete NVIDIA node, one Intel Arc/xe node, and one multi-GPU node; keep the kind CI as regression only and stop counting it as heterogeneity evidence.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited piece of evidence verified. (1) CI is GPU-less: .github/workflows/build.yml:31-33 explicitly says 'GPU-less kind cluster ... skips its GPU-specific assertions' and the e2e job runs only ./hack/scenarios.sh (build.yml:66); scenarios-cpu.sh is not in CI and only hides sysfs (--sys-root=/no-sys, scenarios-cpu.sh:37-39) to collapse inventory to cpu0 — it reduces heterogeneity. (2) GPU scenarios gate on HAS_GPU (scenarios.sh:108-110) and 9/10/11 SKIP without it (scenarios.sh:341, 373-374, 429-430, 487 — all confirmed verbatim). (3) Live cluster confirmed as the only real hardware: resourceslices show exactly cpu0/gpu0/npu0 on one Strix Halo node. (4) NVIDIA/Intel-discrete coverage is fixtures only: publisher_test.go:25 fabricates probe.Device{PCIVendor:"10de", Driver:"nvidia"} and asserts attribute mapping (publisher_test.go:66-72); probe_test.go:26-28 fakes an Intel xe iGPU + intel_vpu in a temp sysfs; multi-GPU exists only as fake-sysfs gpu1 (probe_test.go:105-116). (5) The impact claim survives code reading and strengthens the finding: probe.go:104-176 discovers GPUs solely via /sys/class/drm and sets DevNode=/dev/dri/cardN + RenderNode=/dev/dri/renderD*; nodeplugin/plugin.go:152-157 injects only those DRM nodes into CDI. There is no /dev/nvidia* handling anywhere in the codebase (grep confirms zero non-test hits beyond vendor-name string mapping at publisher.go:213 and llmfit.go:79-80), so on a proprietary-driver NVIDIA node the driver would publish a plausible slice (vendor=nvidia via the 10de map) yet prepare containers lacking /dev/nvidiactl//dev/nvidia0//dev/nvidia-uvm — unusable for CUDA — and no existing test would catch it. Bonus corroboration: even the GPU e2e path hardcodes single-GPU assumptions (scenario 10 asserts LLMFIT_DEVICE = "gpu0" literally, scenarios.sh ~line 421). Mitigations checked and absent: no NVIDIA/Intel node in any test tier, no CI matrix, no compute smoke test beyond device-node existence. Severity: blocker is fair for the stated goal — the owner is claiming heterogeneous (mixed AMD/Intel/NVIDIA, multi-GPU) readiness, and that claim rests entirely on unit-test fixtures while the NVIDIA prepare path is structurally incomplete, not merely untested. It does not block current single-node AMD operation, but it blocks the scale-up claim as stated.

confirmed.title: llmfit-to-probe GPU pairing is vendor-only — wrong capabilities on mixed same-vendor multi-GPU nodes, and no test covers it
confirmed.evidence: matchLLMFitGPU returns the FIRST llmfit GPU whose vendor matches (internal/publisher/publisher.go:164-179: `for i := range sys.GPUs { if llmfit.VendorOf(sys.GPUs[i]) == vendor { return &sys.GPUs[i] } }`). A node with e.g. an AMD 7900 XTX + AMD iGPU gets the same llmfit entry (name, bandwidth, VRAM budget) published for both probed cards. TestBuildDevicesWithLLMFit uses exactly one GPU (publisher_test.go:172-221); no unit test has two same-vendor different-model GPUs; no e2e node has >1 GPU.
confirmed.impact: On heterogeneous multi-GPU nodes the published memoryBandwidthGBs/memory capacity — the exact attributes fit CEL selects on — can be wrong for one of the cards, causing the scheduler to place models on GPUs that cannot hold them. The test blind spot hides a real code bug for the target topology.
confirmed.recommendation: Pair llmfit GPUs to probed devices by PCI address or by (vendor, model-token, VRAM) rather than first-vendor-match; add a unit test with two AMD GPUs of different models asserting each gets its own bandwidth; add a multi-GPU node to the live matrix.

verdict.reasoning: Verified the exact code: internal/publisher/publisher.go:164-179 matchLLMFitGPU returns the first llmfit GPU whose VendorOf (name-substring heuristic, internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:76-90) matches the probed card's vendor, so every same-vendor probed GPU receives the same llmfit entry's model name (publisher.go:98), memoryBandwidthGBs (line 104), and VRAM fit budget (lines 114-115). Attempted refutation via the llmfit submodule made the finding STRONGER: third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/hardware.rs groups GPUs per unique model name (lines 349-412), drops iGPU entries entirely when a dGPU exists via prefer_discrete_gpus (lines 1115-1128), and sorts by VRAM descending (285-289) — so on an AMD dGPU+iGPU node the probed iGPU is published with the dGPU's bandwidth and VRAM. No mitigation exists: the per-device-accurate PCI-ID index bandwidth is only used when the (wrong) llmfit entry lacks bandwidth (publisher.go:105-109), and probe-detected per-device VRAMBytes only when llmfit gave no VRAM (lines 131-137), so wrong llmfit data shadows correct per-device data. Test blind spot confirmed: TestBuildDevicesWithLLMFit (publisher_test.go:172-221) and all other llmfit-path tests use exactly one llmfit GPU; no test has two same-vendor different-model GPUs; the live suite asserts only gpu0 on a single-GPU node (hack/scenarios.sh:104-120). Impact is real for the stated heterogeneous-cluster goal because memoryBandwidthGBs and capacity.memory are exactly what the generated fit CEL selects on. Severity 'major' is correct, not inflated: homogeneous multi-GPU (llmfit count>1) and mixed-vendor nodes are unaffected and current single-GPU deployments work, so it is not a blocker, but common dGPU+iGPU and mixed-dGPU nodes will publish wrong capabilities and misplace workloads. Recommendation (pair by PCI address or vendor+model-token+VRAM; add two-AMD-GPU unit test; add multi-GPU node to live matrix) is sound; note PCI-address pairing requires llmfit to emit PCI identity, which its JSON currently lacks (GPU struct at internal/llmfit/llmfit.go:18-25 has no address field), so (vendor, model-token, VRAM) matching plus refusing ambiguous matches is the practical fix.

confirmed.title: Device-name stability across reboot/hot-remove is untested, and the naming scheme is positional
confirmed.evidence: walkClass assigns Index from a running counter over lexically-sorted directory entries, not from the card number or PCI address (internal/probe/probe.go:150-179: `idx := 0; ... d := Device{... Index: idx ...}; idx++`). So card0+card2 (after a hot-remove) publish as gpu0+gpu1, and the device formerly named gpu1 (card1) silently becomes a different card. Prepare joins allocations to hardware purely by this name (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:103-107). Scenario 12 only asserts a log-line count increment: `grep -c "uevent-triggered re-probe"` before/after (scenarios.sh:494-506) — it never adds/removes a device or asserts the slice changed, let alone that names stayed stable. TestWalkStableAcrossRuns (probe_test.go:217-236) only checks two walks of UNCHANGED hardware are equal.
confirmed.impact: At scale with hotplug or reboots reordering enumeration, an allocated claim ('gpu1') can be prepared against different silicon than the scheduler priced — a silent misplacement, not an error. The one 'hotplug' scenario proves only that the netlink listener wakes the loop.
confirmed.recommendation: Derive device names from PCI address (or publish pciAddress-keyed names) and add: a unit test where card0 disappears and gpu names/identities must not shift, and a live scenario that unbinds/rebinds a driver and asserts slice contents (not log lines) change and names remain stable.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line. probe.go:150-179: walkClass assigns Index from a running counter (idx=0 at :150, Index: idx at :162, idx++ at :179) over lexically-sorted ReadDir entries; the cardN suffix is validated with Atoi but discarded, and PCIAddr (:170) is captured but not used for identity. Name() = kind+Index (probe.go:78-80). nodeplugin/plugin.go:34-57 keys Inventory by Name() (comment: 'Device names (gpu0…) are the join key with allocation results') and prepareClaim joins via inv.lookup(alloc.Device) at :103 with no attribute cross-check; the 'not in current inventory' error at :105-107 only catches the highest-index name vanishing — a middle-card removal shifts names silently (remove card1 from {card0,card1,card2} → gpu1 becomes card2). The shift propagates live: cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go re-probes on uevent and inv.Set(probed) (:116,:144). Publisher exposes pciAddress only as an attribute (publisher.go:79-80) while publishing the positional Name (:141), so scheduler-priced identity is not re-verified at prepare. Test claims accurate: TestWalkStableAcrossRuns (probe_test.go:217-236) walks the same unchanged fake tree twice; no test removes a device and asserts name stability; scenario 12 (hack/scenarios.sh:494-506) only asserts a grep -c log-line increment, never inventory/slice content. Severity: major is right — silent wrong-device preparation, but conditional on multi-device-per-kind nodes plus enumeration change (hot-remove or reboot probe-order reorder with mixed vendors), so not a blocker for the current single-accelerator node, yet directly on the heterogeneous multi-GPU scale-up path and invisible to the existing suite.

confirmed.title: No negative tests: unsatisfiable claims, unhealthy-device rejection, and taint behavior are never exercised end-to-end — and the default deploy allocates unhealthy devices
confirmed.evidence: No scenario creates a claim that must stay Pending (e.g. memoryBandwidthGBs >= 10000) and asserts non-allocation. Health is only unit-tested (probe_test.go:145-187, publisher_test.go:260-291). Taints are opt-in and OFF: --publish-taints defaults false (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:39) and deploy/daemonset.yaml args contain only --probe-interval and --v (daemonset.yaml lines 33-35). The shipped DeviceClasses select only on driver+kind, never healthy (deploy/deviceclass.yaml: `device.driver == "llmfit.ai" && device.attributes[...].kind == "gpu"`).
confirmed.impact: In the default deployment a device published healthy=false (unbound driver, ECC errors) is still fully allocatable by gpu.llmfit.ai claims — prepare would then fail or hand out broken hardware. Nothing in the 12 scenarios or CI would ever detect this; at heterogeneous scale unhealthy devices are a certainty, not an edge case.
confirmed.recommendation: Add e2e negatives: (1) an impossible-CEL claim asserted to remain unallocated with the right Pod condition; (2) a forced-unhealthy device (e.g. fake sys-root with no driver symlink) asserted unschedulable via taint or class-level healthy==true selector. Either enable --publish-taints in the shipped DaemonSet or add healthy to the DeviceClass CEL.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited fact verified against the code and live cluster. (1) hack/scenarios.sh (all 512 lines read): all 12 scenarios are positive-path; none asserts a claim stays unallocated, none forces/tests an unhealthy device, none exercises taints; scenarios-cpu.sh is a wrapper re-running the same suite. (2) Health logic (probe.go:64-75: driverUnbound, uncorrectableEcc) is unit-tested only (probe_test.go:145-187, publisher_test.go:260-298). (3) --publish-taints defaults false (cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go:39); shipped DaemonSet args are only --probe-interval and --v (deploy/daemonset.yaml:31-33); taints only added when opts.Taints (publisher.go:144-149). (4) All four shipped DeviceClasses select only driver+kind, never healthy (deploy/deviceclass.yaml:16-51). Live cluster confirms healthy attr published and taints null on gpu0/npu0/cpu0. One material mitigation the auditor missed: llmfit-claim-generated CEL includes device.attributes['llmfit.ai'].healthy (third_party/llmfit/llmfit-core/src/claim.rs:130-136, tested at :269), so the primary generated-claim workflow (scenarios 9/10) does exclude unhealthy devices at allocation time — the impact statement overstates for that path. But bare class-only claims (exactly what the suite's own apply_consumer and scenarios 4/6 use) allocate unhealthy devices in the default deploy, health is only allocation-time (no eviction without taints), and no e2e/CI test would catch a regression in any of this. Severity major stands for the heterogeneous scale-up goal; one recommendation caveat: enabling --publish-taints in the shipped DaemonSet is gated on the alpha DRADeviceTaints feature gate (documented at publisher.go:29-32), so adding healthy to the DeviceClass CEL is the safer of the two suggested fixes.

confirmed.title: No contention/concurrency coverage — the suite deliberately serializes to need only one device; no -race in CI
confirmed.evidence: Scenario 8 comment: 'Sequential so the suite needs only one allocatable device (cpu0)' (scenarios.sh:331-332); scenarios 6/7/10 explicitly release prior consumers because devices are exclusive (scenarios.sh:244-246,288-291,378-380). No test ever has two pods racing for one device, N claims across N devices, or a pod waiting for a device to free. CI runs plain `go test ./...` with no -race flag (.github/workflows/build.yml:29), so the Inventory RWMutex shared between the re-probe loop and Prepare (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:34-58, main.go:144) is never race-checked.
confirmed.impact: Scheduler queueing under device contention, batch PrepareResourceClaims with many claims, and allocate-while-reprobing races are exactly what a busy heterogeneous cluster produces first; the story has literally zero evidence here.
confirmed.recommendation: Add -race to CI unit tests; add a scenario launching M>devices consumers asserting exactly device-count run and one takes over after a release; add a unit test driving PrepareResourceClaims concurrently with Inventory.Set.

verdict.reasoning: Every factual claim checks out: scenarios.sh:331-332 has the 'Sequential so the suite needs only one allocatable device' comment; scenarios 6/7/10 release prior consumers before re-claiming (scenarios.sh:244-246,288-291,378-380); no scenario among the 12 exercises contention (two pods racing, pending pod waiting on release, N claims across N devices); CI runs plain `go test ./...` (.github/workflows/build.yml:29, also Makefile:10) with no -race anywhere in the repo; the Inventory RWMutex (internal/nodeplugin/plugin.go:34-58) shared between the re-probe writer (inv.Set via desiredState in cmd/llmfit-dra/main.go) and Prepare's lookup is never exercised concurrently by any test (plugin_test.go has zero goroutines/t.Parallel). However, severity is inflated for the scale-up goal: (1) scheduler queueing under device contention is kube-scheduler's DRA allocator's job, not driver code — a contention e2e would mostly test upstream Kubernetes; (2) the driver-owned release→re-claim handoff IS exercised four times (scenarios 6, 7, 8, 10), just without a queued pending pod; (3) the driver's actual concurrent surface is tiny and correct by inspection (whole-map swap under Lock, lookup under RLock, per-claim-UID CDI files, per-claim error isolation in the batch loop at plugin.go:84-90); I ran `go test -race ./...` and all packages pass; (4) adding -race to CI today would verify nothing until a concurrent unit test exists, since no current test runs concurrent paths. The recommendations are cheap, worthwhile regression guards, but this is a test-hygiene gap protecting trivially-correct code, not a likely first failure mode on a heterogeneous cluster — hence minor, not major.

confirmed.title: Scenario 8 is a restart test, not an upgrade test — and with image :main + pullPolicy Always it is not even a controlled restart
confirmed.evidence: Scenario 8 uses `kubectl rollout restart daemonset/llmfit-dra` with no image change (scenarios.sh:315). The DaemonSet ships image ghcr.io/sympozium-ai/llmfit-dra:main with imagePullPolicy: Always (deploy/daemonset.yaml lines 30-31), so a rollout restart can silently pull a different build than the one that prepared the surviving claim. There is no test where version N+1 must honor CDI specs and claims written by version N, and no versioning in the CDI file beyond cdiVersion 0.6.0 (internal/nodeplugin/cdi.go:15-21).
confirmed.impact: In-place upgrades across a large fleet are the first scale operation the owner will perform; cross-version unprepare/attribute-schema compatibility is completely unvalidated, and the floating :main tag makes rollouts non-reproducible per node (different nodes can run different builds).
confirmed.recommendation: Pin the DaemonSet to a digest or semver tag; add an upgrade scenario: deploy tag N, prepare a claim, roll to tag N+1, assert the survivor keeps running and N+1 unprepares N's claim.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all cited evidence. (1) TRUE: Scenario 8 (hack/scenarios.sh:308-336) is a same-image `rollout restart` (line 315); grep confirms no upgrade scenario exists in hack/. (2) TRUE: committed deploy/daemonset.yaml:29-30 ships `ghcr.io/sympozium-ai/llmfit-dra:main` + `imagePullPolicy: Always`, and Makefile:53-56 applies it verbatim, so a manifest-driven fleet deploy uses a floating mutable tag — CI already publishes pinnable sha/semver tags (build.yml:95-100) but the manifest doesn't use them. (3) TRUE: cdi.go:15-21 has only cdiVersion 0.6.0, no driver version stamp. HOWEVER two sub-claims are overstated: (a) "not even a controlled restart" — in both environments where Scenario 8 actually runs the restart IS controlled: CI patches the DaemonSet to :e2e + IfNotPresent before scenarios (build.yml:61-64), and the live cluster runs :dev + IfNotPresent (verified via kubectl get daemonset), so the test itself is deterministic today; the risk applies only to a raw-manifest production deploy. (b) cross-version unprepare risk is largely mitigated by design: removeSpec (cdi.go:86-92) never parses the CDI file, only deletes llmfit.ai-<uid>.json (cdi.go:50-52), with an explicit comment that it may run on a claim a previous instance prepared — N+1 unprepare breaks only if the filename scheme changes. What survives and justifies major for the stated heterogeneous-fleet scale-up: the shipped :main/Always manifest means different nodes pull different builds on any pod restart (non-reproducible rollouts, mixed ResourceSlice attribute schemas across the fleet while llmfit claim CEL is pinned to v0.9.35 attribute names), and there is genuinely no N→N+1 upgrade test covering attribute-schema or filename-scheme stability. Recommendation (pin digest/semver in the manifest; add an upgrade scenario) is correct and cheap.

confirmed.title: Cluster-wide resourceslices write on a shared ServiceAccount lets any compromised node forge/delete every other node's inventory
confirmed.evidence: deploy/rbac.yaml:18-20 grants resource.k8s.io/resourceslices verbs [get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete] in a ClusterRole bound to the single llmfit-dra ServiceAccount (rbac.yaml:31-42). Every DaemonSet pod mounts that SA token. publisher.go:195-206 sets Owner=Node/<nodeName> but that is only an ownerReference for GC, not an authorization boundary. Unlike kubelet identities (system:node:<name>), this generic SA is NOT covered by the NodeRestriction admission plugin, so the apiserver does not constrain which node's slices it may write.
confirmed.impact: At heterogeneous/multi-node scale the blast radius is the whole cluster: one compromised or malicious node's driver pod (which is privileged, so trivially reads the SA token from /var/run/secrets) can create/update/delete ResourceSlices for ANY node — publishing fake healthy GPUs on nodes that have none, deleting real inventory to DoS scheduling, or advertising attacker-favorable attributes to steer claims. This scales from a single-node POC annoyance to a fleet-wide integrity problem.
confirmed.recommendation: Scope the write path per node. Preferred: run the publisher under the node's own kubelet identity or a per-node SA, and rely on/enable the ResourceSlice NodeRestriction (validating admission that a writer may only mutate slices whose spec.nodeName matches its node). If a shared SA is unavoidable, add a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy binding spec.nodeName to the pod's node. Drop list/watch-cluster-wide if only the owning node's slices are needed.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every cited line: deploy/rbac.yaml:18-20 grants get/list/watch/create/update/patch/delete on resource.k8s.io/resourceslices in a ClusterRole bound (rbac.yaml:31-42) to the one llmfit-dra SA that every DaemonSet pod mounts (deploy/daemonset.yaml:17), and those pods are privileged with hostPID/hostNetwork (daemonset.yaml:22,25,40), so a compromised node trivially obtains a token with cluster-wide ResourceSlice write. internal/publisher/publisher.go:195-206 sets Owner{Kind:Node,Name:nodeName} which only drives the ownerReference/GC and pool naming (line 186) — no server-side binding of writer identity to spec.nodeName. The finding's NodeRestriction point is technically correct: the ResourceSlice NodeRestriction extension applies to system:node:* kubelet identities, not generic SAs. No mitigation exists: zero ValidatingAdmissionPolicy manifests in deploy/ (grep) and none installed on the live cluster (kubectl get validatingadmissionpolicies → No resources found). The recommended fix (VAP tying spec.nodeName to the SA token's node claim) is exactly what upstream Kubernetes DRA docs and dra-example-driver ship, confirming this is the expected production hardening. Severity major stands for the heterogeneous multi-node scale-up: blast radius is cluster-wide inventory forgery/deletion and claim-steering, though it requires prior node/pod compromise, so not a blocker. Irrelevant on the current single-node kind cluster, but the audit frame is multi-node.

confirmed.title: privileged: true is broader than any code path requires; CAP_NET_ADMIN + explicit mounts suffice
confirmed.evidence: daemonset.yaml:39-40 sets securityContext.privileged:true. The only elevated operation in the Go code is the netlink socket in hotplug.go:62-67 (needs CAP_NET_ADMIN to join uevent multicast group 1). Everything else is plain filesystem: probe.go reads sysfs/procfs (os.ReadFile/ReadDir), cdi.go writes /var/run/cdi via os.MkdirAll/CreateTemp/Rename, plugin.go writes kubelet plugin sockets. /dev and /sys are already provided as hostPath mounts (daemonset.yaml:42-46,62-67), so device access does not depend on privileged. The bundled llmfit reads /proc/meminfo, /proc/cpuinfo and shells nvidia-smi/rocm-smi/lspci (hardware.rs:308-330,559-582,1636,1933) — none need privileged.
confirmed.impact: privileged grants all capabilities, disables seccomp/AppArmor confinement, and allows access to every host device beyond the mounted ones. Combined with the cluster-wide SA token, a container escape or supply-chain compromise of the image (or the Rust submodule) escalates directly to full host + cluster-wide DRA control on every node running the DaemonSet.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace privileged:true with capabilities.add:[NET_ADMIN], allowPrivilegeEscalation:false, a seccomp RuntimeDefault profile, and keep the explicit /dev, /sys, kubelet, /var/run/cdi mounts. Verify the netlink bind still succeeds (it should with NET_ADMIN).

verdict.reasoning: Attempted refutation failed; every evidence point checks out. (1) deploy/daemonset.yaml:39-40 sets privileged:true; comment at lines 20-21 ('Stage two: full host access') shows it is a blanket grant, not derived from a code requirement. (2) Exhaustive grep of cmd/ and internal/ finds exactly one elevated operation: the NETLINK_KOBJECT_UEVENT socket bind at internal/hotplug/hotplug.go:62-67. probe.go does only sysfs/procfs reads (os.ReadDir/ReadFile/Readlink; lines 140-230, 268, 288) and builds /dev path strings without opening device nodes (173, 176, 214); nodeplugin/cdi.go:57-88 and plugin.go:72 are plain file ops in already-mounted hostPath dirs. No mknod/mount/setns/device-open anywhere. (3) The finding is if anything conservative: kernel uevent netlink sets NL_CFG_F_NONROOT_RECV, so group-1 subscription likely needs no capability at all — only hostNetwork (which daemonset.yaml:23-25 and hotplug.go:8-9 correctly identify); and main.go:102-106 degrades gracefully to ticker-only probing if the bind fails, so privileged is not even load-bearing for hotplug. (4) The device-cgroup counterargument (non-privileged containers can't open GPU nodes even with /dev mounted) does not apply: the runtime image (Dockerfile stage 3) ships only pciutils+llmfit+driver — nvidia-smi/rocm-smi/vulkaninfo calls in hardware.rs (309, 330, 559-572, 1320) fail exec-not-found and llmfit falls back to /sys/class/drm reads (hardware.rs:478, 779, 1203) and lspci sysfs reads; nothing opens device nodes, so dropping privileged breaks nothing. (5) Impact claim verified: deploy/rbac.yaml:19-20 grants cluster-wide create/update/patch/delete on resourceslices via ClusterRoleBinding, so per-node compromise of a privileged fleet-wide DaemonSet escalates to host root + cluster-wide DRA inventory control. Severity 'major' is appropriate for the heterogeneous multi-node scale-up (hardening gap multiplied across every node, not an active vuln, so not 'blocker'). Two caveats for the fix, neither refuting: hostNetwork must be kept (and hostPID:true at daemonset.yaml:22 is a separate unneeded broad grant — llmfit only reads /proc/meminfo and /proc/cpuinfo); and SELinux-enforcing hosts in a mixed fleet will need explicit seLinuxOptions for the hostPath kubelet/CDI writes once the pod no longer runs as spc_t.

confirmed.title: GHCR pull-secret uses a classic PAT passed on the command line (token in process args) with broad scope
confirmed.evidence: Makefile:41-51: GHCR_TOKEN defaults to $(GITHUB_TOKEN) or `gh auth token`, then passed as --docker-password=$(GHCR_TOKEN) to `kubectl create secret docker-registry`. The live cluster secret decodes to a token beginning `ghp_` (classic PAT), confirmed via `kubectl get secret -n llmfit-dra ghcr-pull`. Classic PATs are not scoped to read:packages only — they carry whatever scopes the user granted (often repo, workflow, etc.).
confirmed.impact: Passing the token as a command-line argument exposes it to any local process listing (ps/procfs) and shell history on the operator's machine. A classic PAT stored as a per-namespace pull secret grants far more than image pull if exfiltrated (the secret is readable by anything that can read secrets in llmfit-dra / sympozium-system). At fleet scale the same secret is replicated into every pulling namespace, multiplying exposure.
confirmed.recommendation: Use a fine-grained token or GitHub App installation token scoped to read:packages only, and feed it via stdin/file (kubectl create secret ... --docker-password from a file or `--from-file`) rather than an argv flag. Rotate the existing ghp_ token since it has already been materialized into a secret and command line.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every element of the finding. (1) Makefile:42 sets GHCR_TOKEN from GITHUB_TOKEN or `gh auth token`; Makefile:49 passes it as --docker-password argv to kubectl create secret, run per-namespace over PULL_SECRET_NAMESPACES=llmfit-dra sympozium-system (Makefile:43-51). (2) Live cluster: ghcr-pull docker-registry secrets exist in both namespaces; decoded password is a 40-char ghp_ classic PAT (username AlexsJones). (3) Crucially, I verified the scope claim the auditor only asserted: querying api.github.com with the token from the secret returned x-oauth-scopes: delete:packages, gist, notifications, repo, workflow, write:packages — full repo read/write + workflow + package write/delete, vastly exceeding the read:packages the Makefile comment (lines 37-40) says is needed. (4) deploy/daemonset.yaml:18-19 wires ghcr-pull as imagePullSecrets, so at heterogeneous fleet scale every node kubelet and every secret-reader in those namespaces gets this broad credential; CI (.github/workflows/build.yml:64,83) uses the ephemeral Actions GITHUB_TOKEN and strips imagePullSecrets for its own runs, so nothing mitigates the cluster-side exposure. The argv/procfs sub-claim is the weakest part (recipe is @-prefixed so not echoed; exposure is brief kubectl argv on the operator machine), but the core risk — a repo+workflow-scoped classic PAT materialized into replicated cluster pull secrets — is empirically confirmed. Severity major is appropriate, not inflated; the token should be rotated and replaced with a read:packages-only fine-grained credential.
